title: Aboriginal Australia and the Dreaming
summary: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples have lived on the Australian continent for at least sixty-five thousand years, in hundreds of distinct nations and languages bound to their land by the Dreaming, the sacred web of law, story, and creation. Theirs are the oldest continuing living cultures known. In 1788 the British came to stay, and what followed was both a catastrophe of dispossession, disease, frontier killing, and the forced removal of children, and a long survival and renaissance that continues today.
aliases: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples
The land as law and belonging
An empty land and a useful story

title: Abraham Lincoln
summary: The sixteenth president of the United States, who held the Union together through civil war, issued the Emancipation Proclamation, and was assassinated five days after the Confederate surrender — and whose use of war powers, redefinition of the American founding, and management of an unwinnable political coalition established the modern American presidency.

title: Accounting
summary: The discipline of recording, classifying, and communicating economic activity — and the technology through which power has made the economic world visible to itself. Accounting produced the account, double-entry bookkeeping, the audit, the profession, and the idea of accountability itself, each extending the reach of legibility over economic life.
aliases: Accounting as a System of Choices
Legibility as a Condition of Access
indexTerms: Fridson, Martin S. and Alvarez, Fernando

title: Ada Lovelace
summary: The English mathematician who, working with Charles Babbage's never-built Analytical Engine, published in 1843 what many call the first algorithm written for a machine — and who grasped, more clearly than almost anyone around Babbage, that such a machine could manipulate not just numbers but symbols, music, and ideas, anticipating the general-purpose computer a century before working computers existed.
aliases: The Prophet of the General Machine
associatedWorks: Notes by the Translator (on the Analytical Engine)
Sketch of the Analytical Engine Invented by Charles Babbage
indexTerms: Menabrea, Luigi Federico; Lovelace, Ada (translator and annotator)

title: Adam Smith
summary: Scottish moral philosopher whose Wealth of Nations (1776) provided the foundational analysis of market economies, the division of labor, and the case for free trade — and whose Theory of Moral Sentiments (1759) grounded economic behavior in a broader account of human sympathy and ethics.

title: Adi Shankara
summary: The South Indian philosopher who gave Advaita Vedanta its classical form and Hinduism one of its most influential metaphysical systems, arguing that only one reality, Brahman, truly exists, that the individual self is identical with it, and that the world of separate things is appearance. His commentaries on the Upanishads, the Bhagavad Gita, and the Brahma Sutras made Advaita Vedanta one of the most influential intellectual articulations of the tradition.

title: African Independence (Year of Africa, 1960)
summary: The rapid wave of decolonization that, in barely two decades after World War II, dismantled European colonial rule across most of Africa — symbolized by 1960, the 'Year of Africa,' when seventeen countries became independent in a single year — restoring African sovereignty and raising soaring hopes, while leaving the new states the colonial borders, extractive economies, and fragile institutions that shaped their struggles ever after.

title: African Socialism
summary: The family of political ideologies that influenced many newly independent African states from the 1950s to the 1970s, holding that socialism was not a foreign import but the recovery of communal values already present in pre-colonial African life — a mid-century attempt to build a development path that was neither Western capitalism nor Soviet communism.
aliases: The View From Under One-Party Rule
indexTerms: Friedland, William H. and Rosberg, Carl G. (eds.)
William H. Friedland and Carl G. Rosberg (eds.)

title: Akbar
summary: The third Mughal emperor, who inherited a shaky North Indian realm at thirteen and turned it into one of the great powers of the early-modern world. Uniquely among the Muslim rulers of India, he made religious conciliation a principle of state — abolishing the tax on non-Muslims, allying with Hindu Rajput clans, and setting the throne above every faith — while remaining a conquering, sometimes ruthless emperor whose armies sacked the fortress of Chittor.
aliases: The emperor who unsettled the faith
The symbol pulled in two directions
indexTerms: Abu'l-Fazl (trans. H. Blochmann and H.S. Jarrett)

title: Akira Kurosawa
summary: The Japanese filmmaker who directed some thirty films across half a century and is widely regarded as one of the most influential directors in the history of cinema. His Rashomon (1950) won the top prize at Venice and brought Japanese cinema to wide Western attention, while Seven Samurai (1954) became a template that filmmakers everywhere borrowed. His samurai pictures were remade as Hollywood Westerns and his techniques shaped directors from Sergio Leone to George Lucas, even as critics at home for periods attacked him as too Western to be truly Japanese.

title: Aksum
summary: The ancient kingdom of northern Ethiopia and Eritrea that became one of the ancient world's great trading powers, one of the first states to adopt Christianity as a state religion, and the origin point of a living tradition — the Ethiopian Orthodox Church — that claims an unbroken line to the fourth century.

title: Al-Andalus
summary: The Muslim-ruled lands of the Iberian Peninsula between the conquest of 711 and the fall of Granada in 1492 — a medieval civilization of great cities, libraries, irrigation, philosophy, and poetry, where Muslims, Christians, and Jews lived together in an arrangement that was productive and unequal at once, and whose memory still serves as a contested symbol of coexistence lost.

title: Al-Biruni
summary: The Khwarezmian polymath who measured the Earth's size with a single mountain and a careful angle, and who travelled into India in the entourage of a conqueror only to produce a calm, comparative study of Hindu religion, science, and custom that many modern scholars treat as an early model of cross-cultural inquiry — a thinker whose central discipline was distinguishing what he had verified from what he had merely been told.
associatedWorks: Book on the Determination of Coordinates of Cities
Book of Instruction in the Elements of the Art of Astrology
indexTerms: al-Biruni, Abu Rayhan; Sachau, Edward C. (trans.)
Abu Rayhan al-Biruni (trans. Edward C. Sachau)

title: Al-Farabi
summary: The tenth-century philosopher whom the Islamic tradition honored as 'the Second Teacher' — second only to Aristotle — for systematizing Greek logic in Arabic and working within the tradition that read Plato and Aristotle as fundamentally in harmony. His emanationist metaphysics and his Platonic vision of a virtuous city led by a ruler who joins philosophical understanding to prophetic imagination set the agenda for Islamic philosophy, shaping Ibn Sina directly and reaching, through translation, into Jewish and Christian thought.
associatedWorks: The Principles of the Opinions of the Inhabitants of the Virtuous City
indexTerms: Jon McGinnis and David C. Reisman

title: Al-Ghazali
summary: The Persian jurist, theologian, and mystic who, at the height of his fame, abandoned a prestigious teaching post in Baghdad for a decade of withdrawal — and whose two greatest works, a sharp critique of the Aristotelian philosophers and a vast synthesis of law with Sufi spirituality, reshaped mainstream Sunni Islam, making mysticism respectable and setting the terms on which faith and reason would be argued for centuries.
aliases: The Bridge to the Inner Path
associatedWorks: The Revival of the Religious Sciences

title: Al-Khwarizmi
summary: The ninth-century Persian scholar at Baghdad's House of Wisdom whose treatise on al-jabr gave algebra its name and its method, whose name became the word 'algorithm,' and who helped carry the Hindu-Arabic decimal system into the Islamic world and eventually into Europe — making him one of the most consequential mathematicians in history, despite almost nothing being known about his life.
aliases: A Product of the Translation Movement
associatedWorks: Kitab al-mukhtasar fi hisab al-jabr wal-muqabala

title: Al-Razi (Rhazes)
summary: The Persian physician of ninth- and tenth-century Rayy and Baghdad, Latinized as Rhazes, who ran hospitals, kept careful records of his patients, and wrote one of the earliest clear clinical accounts distinguishing smallpox from measles. His vast medical compendium, the Hawi or Continens, carried his case-based, observational style into the European universities, while his willingness to test Galen against his own findings made him one of the medieval world's sharpest clinical empiricists — and attacks on prophecy attributed to him by hostile opponents made him one of its most controversial figures.
associatedWorks: Kitab al-Hawi fi al-Tibb (The Comprehensive Book / Continens)
Kitab al-Judari wa al-Hasba (On Smallpox and Measles)
indexTerms: al-Razi, Abu Bakr Muhammad ibn Zakariya
Peter E. Pormann and Emilie Savage-Smith
Abu Bakr Muhammad ibn Zakariya al-Razi (trans. W. A. Greenhill)

title: Alan Turing
summary: The British mathematician who founded theoretical computer science, broke the Enigma cipher during the Second World War, and asked 'Can machines think?' — defining the questions that artificial intelligence has pursued ever since — before being prosecuted for homosexuality and dying at 41.
aliases: The Pioneer of Questions About Mind
associatedWorks: On Computable Numbers, with an Application to the Entscheidungsproblem

title: Albert Einstein
summary: The German-born theoretical physicist whose special relativity (1905) and general relativity (1915) overturned Newtonian mechanics and transformed the understanding of space, time, gravity, and the cosmos — making him the most recognizable scientist in history and a cultural symbol of intellectual genius.
associatedWorks: On the Electrodynamics of Moving Bodies
indexTerms: Abbott, B. P. et al. (LIGO Scientific Collaboration and Virgo Collaboration)

title: Alexander the Great
summary: The Macedonian king who conquered the largest contiguous empire in ancient history in just thirteen years -- from Greece to Egypt to Persia to the edge of India -- and whose campaigns spread Greek culture across the Near East in ways that shaped the ancient world for centuries.

title: Alexandria
summary: The city Alexander the Great founded at the mouth of the Nile in 331 BCE — which became the ancient world's greatest center of knowledge, the meeting point of Greek, Egyptian, Jewish, and later Christian and Islamic intellectual traditions, and the site of the most famous library ever destroyed
aliases: Hypatia and the End of Pagan Philosophy
The Bibliotheca Alexandrina and the Politics of Revival
indexTerms: Translators of the Septuagint (traditionally the Seventy)

title: Aluminum
summary: The most abundant metal in the Earth's crust, yet hidden in ore so tightly bound that for most of history no one could extract it cheaply — once more precious than gold, now so cheap we throw it away — aluminum is the metal that electricity made, a parable of energy as the true hidden cost of modern materials.
indexTerms: Barham, Bradford, Stephen Bunker, and Denis O'Hearn (eds.)

title: Amazonian Earthworks
summary: The geometric ditched enclosures, raised fields, causeways, mounds, and dark fertile soils found across parts of the Amazon basin — physical evidence that the rainforest, long imagined as empty wilderness, was home before 1492 to large and complex human societies that shaped the forest itself, and one of the most debated discoveries in American archaeology.
aliases: A Landscape Read From Ditches and Soil
A Deep Presence, Not an Empty Forest

title: Amílcar Cabral
summary: The agronomist, guerrilla leader, and political theorist who led the independence struggle of Portuguese Guinea and Cape Verde, and who argued that liberation must be a 'return to the source' of indigenous culture — demanding that the Westernized elite commit 'class suicide' to serve the people, insisting that revolutionaries 'tell no lies, claim no easy victories,' and building schools, clinics, and courts in liberated zones even as he fought, before his assassination in 1973 months before the independence he had made inevitable.

title: Amritsar
summary: The holiest city of the Sikhs, built around a sacred pool and the Golden Temple — a low shrine open on all four sides to people of every caste and faith — which in the twentieth century became the stage for two of the deepest wounds in modern Indian history: the 1919 Jallianwala Bagh massacre and the 1984 army assault on the temple complex.

title: Ancient Egypt
summary: The civilization that arose from the Nile's predictable floods — organized around the most reliable agricultural system in the ancient world, producing the administrative, artistic, and engineering achievements that made Egypt a continuous civilization for three thousand years and a model for every subsequent empire that encountered it
aliases: Two River Systems, Two Political Structures
Egypt and the Question of African Civilization

title: Angkor
summary: The vast medieval capital of the Khmer Empire in what is now Cambodia — a sprawling low-density city built around an engineered network of reservoirs and canals, home to Angkor Wat and the Bayon, abandoned as a royal seat in the 15th century, rediscovered by Europeans in the 19th, and now both Cambodia's national symbol and one of Asia's most visited ruins.
aliases: A Temple That Was Never Lost
The Monument Outsiders Tried to Claim
A City Engineered to Its Own Edge
The Flag and the Golden Age
indexTerms: Michael D. Coe and Damian Evans
Damian Evans, Roland Fletcher, Christophe Pottier, et al.

title: Angkor Wat
summary: The largest religious monument on earth — a single sandstone temple in Cambodia, raised in the early twelfth century under King Suryavarman II as a scale model of the Hindu cosmos and dedicated to the god Vishnu. Built for a god and most likely for a king's own afterlife, it outlived the empire that made it, was converted from Hindu to Theravada Buddhist worship, was never fully abandoned, and became the soul and the flag of the Cambodian nation.
aliases: The Living Heart of the Nation
The Wonder Outsiders Tried to Claim
A Model of the Universe That Changed Its Faith
indexTerms: Coe, Michael D., and Damian Evans
Michael D. Coe and Damian Evans

title: Antibiotics
summary: Compounds that kill or inhibit the growth of bacteria, first developed from natural mold extracts in the 1920s–1940s and now among the most widely used medicines in human history — responsible for saving hundreds of millions of lives, enabling modern surgery and cancer treatment, and generating the crisis of antimicrobial resistance that threatens to reverse their gains.
indexTerms: Laxminarayan, Ramanan; Duse, Adriano; et al.
Industry Roadmap signatories (World Economic Forum, Davos)

title: Antoine Lavoisier
summary: The French chemist who helped transform chemistry into a quantitative science by weighing everything before and after a reaction — establishing the law of conservation of mass, naming oxygen and overthrowing the phlogiston theory of combustion, and co-creating modern chemical nomenclature — before being guillotined in 1794 as a former royal tax collector during the Terror of the French Revolution.
aliases: The Founder of a Quantitative Science

title: Apartheid
summary: The system of institutionalized racial segregation and white minority rule in South Africa, formally in force from 1948 to 1994, which classified every person by race, organized every aspect of social and economic life accordingly, suppressed Black political resistance with systematic violence, and ended through negotiated transition after decades of struggle.

title: Apartheid Resistance
summary: The decades-long struggle, roughly 1948 to 1994, through which South Africa's Black majority and their allies fought to overturn apartheid — moving through petition, mass civil disobedience, armed struggle, township insurrection, and a global pressure campaign of sanctions and boycotts, and winning not a clean military victory but a negotiated revolution that left the country free, democratic, and still burdened by apartheid's economic legacy.

title: Archimedes
summary: The Greek mathematician and engineer of Syracuse, among the greatest of antiquity, who measured the circle by trapping it between polygons, found the area and volume of curved figures by a method of exhaustion that anticipated the integral calculus, founded the sciences of statics and hydrostatics with the law of the lever and the principle of buoyancy, devised the water-raising screw and compound pulley, and was killed by a Roman soldier during the sack of Syracuse in 212 BC.
aliases: The Mind at the Edge of the Calculus
associatedWorks: On the Sphere and the Cylinder

title: Aristotle
summary: The philosopher who invented the categories through which Western and global intellectual culture organized knowledge for two millennia

title: Artificial Intelligence
summary: The project of building machines that can perform tasks requiring human-like intelligence — from its founding in 1956 through symbolic AI, expert systems, and multiple winters, to the deep learning revolution of the 2010s and the large language model era — now the most rapidly developing technology in human history and the subject of the most consequential unresolved questions about the future of work, knowledge, and civilization.
indexTerms: Krizhevsky, Alex; Sutskever, Ilya; Hinton, Geoffrey

title: Ashoka
summary: Emperor of the Maurya Empire who conquered most of the Indian subcontinent, was transformed by the violence of his wars, converted to Buddhism, and then ruled according to dhamma — a code of ethical governance — leaving behind the earliest major inscriptions in Indian history and one of the most influential examples of a ruler renouncing conquest.
aliases: The Political Strategist Who Was Also Genuinely Transformed

title: Asphalt
summary: The black, sticky petroleum residue — bitumen mixed with crushed stone — that paves most of the world's roads, the cheap and repairable surface that made the automobile age physically possible, and a byproduct of oil refining so thoroughly spread across the planet that we now walk, drive, and build on it without noticing it is there.
aliases: The Landscape We Chose and Can Reconsider
The Practical Surface That Connected the Country
The Surface That Changed the Water and the Heat

title: Ataturk and the Founding of the Turkish Republic
summary: The Ottoman army officer who led the Turkish War of Independence, abolished the sultanate and caliphate, and founded the Republic of Turkey in 1923 as its first president. Through sweeping top-down reforms he built a secular, Western-facing nation-state on the ruins of a multiethnic empire. His achievements and his authoritarian, assimilationist methods are both part of the record, and both are still argued over.

title: Athenian Democracy
summary: The system of direct self-government developed in Athens from Cleisthenes' reforms of 508/7 BCE through the death of Demosthenes in 322 BCE — the first sustained experiment in democratic rule, foundational to Western political thought, and far stranger and more radical than most modern appropriations acknowledge.
indexTerms: Hamilton, Alexander; Madison, James; Jay, John

title: Athens
summary: The city that became Western civilization's origin myth — and the historical reality that the myth requires not looking at too carefully

title: Atomic Theory
summary: The idea that matter is composed of indivisible fundamental units — atoms — proposed by Greek philosophers, abandoned for two millennia, revived by chemists in the 19th century, transformed by physicists in the 20th, and now the foundation of all chemistry, much of physics, and the technology of nuclear weapons and nuclear power.
aliases: What Quantum Mechanics Says About Reality
indexTerms: Sagan, Scott D.; Waltz, Kenneth N.

title: Attila and the Huns
summary: The steppe horseman who, in a few terrifying years, made himself the nightmare of the dying Roman world, extorted the Eastern Empire, threatened the West, and rode almost unchecked from the Danube into northern Italy, becoming for fifteen centuries the West's very image of the barbarian at the gate, even though he never took a great city or left a lasting state behind him.

title: Augustine of Hippo
summary: North African theologian and philosopher whose Confessions and City of God shaped Western Christianity's understanding of sin, grace, free will, time, and the relationship between the church and political power — making him the most influential Christian thinker between Paul and the Reformation.

title: Aung San
summary: The founding father of independent Burma, who led his country to the threshold of freedom from British rule and was assassinated at thirty-two, six months before independence arrived — a student radical who built the Burmese army, made a wartime bargain with Japan and then turned against it, negotiated independence with London, and tried at Panglong to bind Burma's frontier leaders — Shan, Kachin, and Chin — into a future union that other groups, including many Karen, stood outside, leaving behind both the nation and the army that would later rule it as a dictatorship, and a counterfactual that still haunts Myanmar.
aliases: The Father of the Nation and the Army

title: Authoritarianism
summary: The political system or disposition in which power is concentrated in a single leader or small group, political opposition is suppressed, and citizens are subordinated to the state — a category that encompasses regimes from military juntas to competitive autocracies, and a problem that democracy has never solved.
indexTerms: Adorno, Theodor W., Else Frenkel-Brunswik, Daniel J. Levinson and R. Nevitt Sanford
Hibbing, John R., Kevin B. Smith and John R. Alford
V-Dem Institute (Varieties of Democracy), University of Gothenburg

title: Averroes / Ibn Rushd
summary: The Cordoban jurist, physician, and philosopher who spent his life trying to recover what he saw as the authentic Aristotle from the Neoplatonist overlay he believed earlier Muslim thinkers had added, and who answered al-Ghazali's attack on philosophy with a defense of reason as a path to the same truth as revelation — a body of work whose Latin and Hebrew afterlife was far larger than its immediate Islamic philosophical reception, though recent scholarship shows his works did not simply vanish from Muslim intellectual life.
associatedWorks: Long Commentary on Aristotle's De Anima
The Exposition of the Methods of Proof

title: Aviation
summary: Powered, controlled flight — first achieved at Kitty Hawk in 1903 and scaled within a single human lifetime into a global network of jet routes carrying billions of passengers a year. Aviation shrank the planet from weeks to hours, restructured warfare, enabled mass tourism and migration, and built the fast lane of globalization in air freight — while generating roughly three percent of global warming emissions and remaining dependent on state subsidy and military procurement.
aliases: Aviation as an Instrument of Civilian Terror
Aviation and the Geography of Global Production

title: Ayurveda
summary: The classical medical tradition of the Indian subcontinent, whose Sanskrit name means "the science of life" (ayus, life, and veda, knowledge). Rooted in the late Vedic world and systematized in great compendia such as the Charaka Samhita and the Sushruta Samhita, it holds that health is the balance of three bodily humors, the doshas, and treats imbalance through diet, herbs, lifestyle, oil therapies, and purification. One of the world's oldest organized medical systems and a genuine ancient achievement, it is also a booming global wellness industry whose specific therapeutic claims mostly lack robust clinical support, and some of whose traditional metal-based preparations have caused heavy-metal poisoning.
aliases: A Knowledge System of Its Time
The Question of Proof and Safety
indexTerms: Dagmar Wujastyk and Frederick M. Smith (editors)

title: Ayutthaya
summary: The island capital of Siam for over four centuries, set where three rivers meet in central Thailand. A cosmopolitan trading port that European visitors ranked among the greatest cities of the world, ringed by canals and crowded with gilded temples and corn-cob towers, it was captured and burned by a Burmese army in 1767. The kingdom regrouped downriver at what became Bangkok, and Ayutthaya was never rebuilt as Siam's capital; its brick ruins and root-wrapped Buddha head are now a UNESCO World Heritage Site and a Thai memory of lost grandeur.
aliases: The Great Victory in the West
The Capital That Moved to the Sea
indexTerms: Richard D. Cushman (trans.) and David K. Wyatt (ed.)
Chris Baker, Dhiravat na Pombejra, Alfons van der Kraan, and David K. Wyatt
Richard D. Cushman (trans.), David K. Wyatt (ed.)

title: B.R. Ambedkar
summary: The Dalit scholar, jurist, and political leader who designed the Indian constitution, mounted the most powerful intellectual challenge to Gandhi's approach to caste, converted to Buddhism with hundreds of thousands of followers, and whose arguments about structural inequality and the limits of reform from within remain the foundational texts of Dalit liberation thought.
aliases: The Global Theorist of Inherited Inequality

title: Babur
summary: The Central Asian prince who lost his ancestral kingdom and won an empire instead — descended from both Timur and Genghis Khan, driven out of Samarkand by the Uzbeks, he seized Kabul, then conquered northern India at Panipat in 1526 and founded the Mughal dynasty. He also wrote the Baburnama, one of the most candid memoirs any conqueror has left.

title: Bagan
summary: The capital of the first kingdom to unify much of what is now Myanmar — a dusty plain along the Irrawaddy River where, over roughly two and a half centuries, Buddhist kings, queens, and commoners raised thousands of brick temples and stupas to earn merit toward a better rebirth. By tradition some ten thousand were built; more than two thousand still stand, making Bagan one of the densest concentrations of religious architecture ever assembled and the cradle of Burmese Buddhist civilization.
aliases: A Plain Built for the Next Life

title: Baghdad
summary: The city founded in 762 CE by the Abbasid caliph al-Mansur on the Tigris as a planned circular capital, which grew into one of the largest and richest cities in the medieval world and the center of the Islamic Golden Age, before the Mongol sack of 1258 and centuries as a provincial town — and which is now the capital of Iraq.
aliases: The City Built to Be the Center

title: Bananas
summary: The world's most exported fresh fruit is a sterile, seedless clone — commercial Cavendish bananas are propagated clonally from a single narrow genetic base, making the global supply chain a monoculture so fragile that a single fungus nearly destroyed it once and threatens to do so again. Behind the cheap, familiar fruit lies the history of the United Fruit Company, the coining of 'banana republic,' and the CIA-backed coup that toppled Guatemala's elected government in 1954 — a reminder that the politics of cheap food are never simple.

title: Banking
summary: The institutional practice of holding deposits, extending credit, and transferring money across space and time — a foundation of economic civilization so pervasive that its absence is almost unimaginable, and whose periodic crises reveal how much of modern life is built on organized debt.
indexTerms: Kindleberger, Charles P. and Robert Aliber
Reinhart, Carmen M. and Kenneth S. Rogoff
North, Douglass C. and Barry R. Weingast
Bank of England Monetary Analysis Directorate
Carmen M. Reinhart and Kenneth S. Rogoff

title: Bankruptcy
summary: The legal procedure through which societies decide who loses how much when debts cannot be paid — converting the old sin of insolvency from a personal disgrace into an orderly process, and balancing the honest debtor's claim to a fresh start against the creditor's claim to be repaid.
aliases: The Fresh Start as a Social Good

title: Bartolomé de las Casas
summary: The Spanish colonist who changed sides. Las Casas came to the Caribbean as a young settler, held a grant of forced Indigenous labor, and then underwent a conversion so complete that he spent the rest of his long life as the fiercest enemy of the empire that had made him. He indicted the conquest of the Americas as one of history's great crimes, argued that the peoples of the New World were fully human and fully free, and became a founding ancestor of the idea of universal human rights — even as his own record carried a grave contradiction he later renounced: his early proposal to import enslaved Africans to relieve the Indians.
associatedWorks: A Short Account of the Destruction of the Indies

title: Bauhaus
summary: The radical German art-and-design school founded by the architect Walter Gropius in Weimar in 1919, which in just fourteen years fused fine art, craft, and the machine into a new creed: that everything from a teapot to a skyscraper should be honest, functional, and made for the modern age. Born in the ruins of the First World War, it taught painting, furniture, metalwork, weaving, typography, theater, and architecture together, with a faculty that included Paul Klee, Wassily Kandinsky, and Laszlo Moholy-Nagy. Its core creed -- a functionalist principle it summarized as "form follows function" (a phrase it borrowed from Louis Sullivan), that good design strips away ornament to serve use, and that well-made objects mass-produced could improve everyday life -- became the foundation of modern design. The Nazis, who hated its modernist and leftist spirit, forced it to close in 1933, scattering its masters across the world. Its principles became the glass-and-steel International Style and the basis of modern graphic, product, and furniture design, though critics later charged that this style hardened into a cold, placeless corporate orthodoxy.
aliases: The Glass Box and Its Discontents

title: Beijing
summary: The capital of China for six centuries and the center of the world's most continuously inhabited civilization — a city whose built environment embeds a complete cosmological and political order and whose contemporary significance is inseparable from the authoritarian state that now occupies it.
aliases: The Capital of the People's Republic
The Forbidden City as World Heritage

title: Benin Bronzes
summary: Thousands of brass and bronze plaques, heads, and figures cast by the Edo people for the royal court of the Kingdom of Benin, in present-day Edo State, Nigeria, from at least the sixteenth century, within a court casting tradition whose roots may reach earlier. Made largely by lost-wax casting in brass for the palace of the Oba, they recorded court history and ritual in metal. In 1897 a British military expedition sacked Benin City and looted the works, which were sold across Europe and America. They are now the central case in the global debate over returning objects taken in the colonial era.

title: Benin Kingdom
summary: The Kingdom of Benin (the Edo state in what is now southern Nigeria, not the modern country called Benin) was one of West Africa's most sophisticated and long-lived kingdoms — ruled by a divine king called the Oba, ringed by vast earthwork walls, and famous above all for the cast-brass and bronze artworks whose looting by a British force in 1897 became a defining case in the global debate over returning stolen heritage.
aliases: The objects as world art and museum foundation
Benin as a test case for everyone else

title: Beowulf
summary: The Old English epic poem preserved in a single manuscript from around 1000 CE, recounting the Geatish hero Beowulf's battles against the monster Grendel, Grendel's mother, and a dragon. Beowulf is the oldest surviving long poem in English, the foundational text of English literary tradition, and a document of the Germanic heroic world that shaped medieval Scandinavia and England. Its reception history — from obscurity through nineteenth-century nationalism to Seamus Heaney's landmark 2000 translation — is as significant as the poem itself.
aliases: The Poem on Its Own Terms
indexTerms: R.D. Fulk, Robert E. Bjork, John D. Niles

title: Bob Marley
summary: The Jamaican singer and songwriter who, between the late 1960s and his death in 1981, shaped reggae into a global language of liberation, carrying the Rastafari faith's message of Black pride and resistance to Babylon across every continent, surviving an assassination attempt and brokering an onstage truce in Jamaica's gang-torn politics, playing at Zimbabwe's independence, and becoming in a short life the first worldwide superstar of the developing world and a lasting symbol of freedom for the oppressed.
aliases: The voice of the liberated diaspora

title: Bollywood
summary: Bollywood is the Hindi-language commercial film industry of Mumbai (Bombay), one branch of the world's largest national cinema. From the silent feature of 1913 through the song-and-dance 'masala' blockbuster of today, it became one of the most-watched storytelling machines on earth and a binding imagination for a subcontinent of many languages and faiths. The nickname blends Bombay with Hollywood, and many in the industry resent it: far from a Hollywood imitation, Mumbai's Hindi cinema grew from India's own theatrical, musical, and literary traditions as much as from global film. The films are long, star-driven, and built on songs that the actors mime to playback singers. Those songs are the popular music of much of South Asia. At its center sits a rare space of Hindu-Muslim collaboration and a shared, Urdu-inflected 'Hindustani' culture, now negotiating a louder religious nationalism, debates over star dynasties, and the pull of a global diaspora audience.
aliases: The Cinema That Imagined a Nation

title: Borders
summary: A border is the line where one sovereign's authority ends and another's begins — and the site of a deep moral argument over who may draw it, cross it, and be excluded by it. Borders organize the modern world into territorial states, turn accidents of birth into lifelong allocations of rights, and generate one of the most genuinely contested debates in contemporary ethics: whether any government has the right to exclude outsiders, and on what grounds.

title: Borobudur
summary: The largest surviving Buddhist monument on earth — a colossal stepped pyramid of volcanic stone on the island of Java, in central Indonesia, raised around 780 to 840 CE under the Sailendra dynasty as a three-dimensional map of the path to enlightenment. Not a temple you enter but a mountain you ascend, it is faced with one of the world's most extensive surviving ensembles of Buddhist reliefs and crowned by a great stupa. Abandoned and half-buried for centuries, it was cleared to Western attention in 1814 and rebuilt by a major restoration campaign, and it stands today, a wonder of the world, in a Muslim-majority nation.
aliases: A Path to Enlightenment Built in Stone
The Wonder Outsiders Cleared and Claimed
A Buddhist Treasure of a Muslim-Majority Nation
indexTerms: Gómez, Luis O., and Hiram W. Woodward, eds.
Luis O. Gómez and Hiram W. Woodward (eds.)

title: Brick
summary: The fired or sun-dried clay unit — hand-sized, standardized, and stackable — that is among the oldest manufactured building materials on earth, and whose mass production from Mesopotamia to the Indus Valley, from Roman cities to the industrial age, has always depended on the muscle of the people who made and laid it.
aliases: The Hidden Cost in the Wall
indexTerms: Campbell, James W.P. and Will Pryce
International Labour Organization and Walk Free Foundation
James W.P. Campbell and Will Pryce

title: British Honduras
summary: The colonial extraction settlement on the Caribbean coast of Central America that became modern Belize. Conjured out of a single tree, it began as a cluster of English logcutters, some with buccaneering pasts, felling logwood for textile dye, then shifted to mahogany cut by enslaved Africans deep in the forest. Claimed by Spain but never made a dense settler colony, formalized by Britain only in 1862, and contested by Guatemala to this day, it left behind an English-speaking, plural society wedged into Spanish-speaking Central America.
aliases: A Settlement of Free Men Defended Against an Empire
indexTerms: Nancy Marguerite Anderson and the editors of the Handbook of Middle American Indians

title: Bronze
summary: The first major engineered metal alloy — copper alloyed with tin to produce something harder and more castable than either ingredient alone — whose adoption organized an age of long-distance trade, specialized elites, and palace kingdoms, and whose power was always hostage to the trade routes that carried its rarest ingredient.
indexTerms: Timothy Earle and Michael E. Smith

title: Buddhism
summary: The religious and philosophical tradition founded on the teachings of Siddhartha Gautama — the Buddha — in northeastern India around the fifth century BCE, whose analysis of suffering and the path to its cessation became one of the most consequential intellectual traditions in world history, spreading across Asia and now globally.

title: Bukhara
summary: The holy heart of Central Asia — a Silk Road oasis city so dense with mosques, madrasas, and saints that Muslims called it Bukhara the Noble, the brilliant capital of a Persianate golden age and a thousand-year center of Islamic learning, which in the modern age became, in Western imperial memory, a byword for a closed and cruel emirate.

title: Bureaucracy
summary: The system of administration through specialized officials, written records, hierarchical authority, and impersonal rules — independently invented by every large civilization in history, from Han China to the Roman Empire to the Ottoman devshirme to the modern nation-state — and the concept through which Max Weber understood modernity itself: the 'iron cage' of rationalized, impersonal rule that modern societies cannot escape.

title: Cacao
summary: The bitter bean of a tropical American tree (Theobroma cacao, 'food of the gods'), processed by Mesoamerican peoples into a spiced, frothy drink and used in Postclassic and contact-period Mesoamerica, by the Maya and the Aztecs, as money, tribute, and sacred substance — cacao crossed to Europe after the conquest, was sweetened into the chocolate the modern world eats, and now rests on a global cacao belt whose West African farms carry documented problems of poverty and child labor.
aliases: The Bean That Was Money and Sacrament
From Bitter Drink to Sweet Bar
indexTerms: Coe, Sophie D. and Coe, Michael D.
Martin, Carla D. and Sampeck, Kathryn E.
Sophie D. Coe and Michael D. Coe

title: Cahokia
summary: The largest city in the Americas north of Mexico before European contact — a planned earthwork metropolis near modern St. Louis that rose around 1050 CE, held tens of thousands of people, and was abandoned within a few centuries, complicating the story that pre-contact North America held no cities.
aliases: The City Read From the Ground
A Living Heritage, Not a Lost City
The City That Did Not Fit the Story
indexTerms: Biloine Whiting Young and Melvin L. Fowler

title: Cairo
summary: The city a Shia dynasty founded in 969 CE beside the Nile and named al-Qahira, 'the Victorious' — which grew into the largest and richest metropolis of the medieval Islamic world, home to the al-Azhar mosque and its thousand-year center of learning, and is today the capital of Egypt and the largest city in the Arab world.
aliases: The City That Survived the Mongols
The Hinge of the Spice Trade
The Capital of the Arab Imagination

title: Camels
summary: The living technology of the desert — the camel opened the Sahara and the arid corridors of the Silk Road to caravan commerce, connecting sub-Saharan Africa to the Mediterranean world and Central Asia to China across terrain that horses, oxen, and wheeled carts could not cross. Historian Richard Bulliet's argument that camels actually displaced the wheel across much of the Middle East and North Africa for roughly a millennium captures the animal's deeper significance: it did not merely supplement existing transport but reorganized it entirely.
aliases: The Desert Belongs to Those Who Know It
When the Animal Beats the Machine
indexTerms: Ibn Battuta, translated by H.A.R. Gibb

title: Capital
summary: Karl Marx's three-volume analysis of the capitalist mode of production, published from 1867 to 1894, which provided the theoretical foundation for socialist and communist movements worldwide. Capital argued that capitalism systematically exploits labour through the extraction of surplus value, that this exploitation is structural rather than incidental, and that capitalism contains internal contradictions that will produce its eventual crisis. The book's world effect ran primarily through the states and movements it founded rather than through academic economics.

title: Capitalism
summary: The economic system in which the means of production are privately owned, goods and services are allocated through markets, and profit-seeking drives investment — a system that has generated unprecedented material wealth, lifted billions from poverty, and produced inequality, environmental destruction, and periodic crises that have generated the most consequential political conflicts of the modern era.
indexTerms: Hall, Peter A. and Soskice, David (eds.)

title: Captain Cook's Pacific Voyages
summary: The three voyages of the British navigator James Cook between 1768 and 1779, which transformed European mapping of the Pacific more than any earlier British expedition and filled in the last great blank on the European map of the world. Cook mapped New Zealand and the eastern coast of Australia, crossed deep into Antarctic waters, reached Hawai'i, and advanced science from astronomy to the near-defeat of scurvy at sea. The same voyages were the leading edge of European expansion into the Pacific, opening Australia, New Zealand, and the islands to colonization, settlement, and devastating disease among peoples who had lived there for centuries or millennia. Cook was killed at Kealakekua Bay in Hawai'i in 1779.
aliases: The Beginning of the End of Isolation

title: Caral / Norte Chico
summary: The ruined city of Caral, in the Supe Valley of coastal Peru, is the centerpiece of the Norte Chico (Caral-Supe) civilization, which flourished from around 3000 BCE. It is the showpiece of Caral-Supe / Norte Chico, widely recognized as the oldest known center of civilization in the Americas, roughly contemporary with the pyramids of Egypt and the first cities of Sumer. Its people raised large platform mounds and sunken circular plazas, yet left no pottery and almost no images of war. Its dating, its reputation as a peaceful society, and its place among the world's earliest cradles of civilization are all debated.
aliases: The Root of a Three-Thousand-Year Tradition
indexTerms: Ruth Shady Solís and Carlos Leyva (eds.)
Ruth Shady Solís, Jonathan Haas, and Winifred Creamer

title: Carl Friedrich Gauss
summary: The German mathematician often called the Prince of Mathematicians, who reshaped number theory, geometry, astronomy, and the science of measurement. Born poor in Brunswick in 1777, Gauss published the 'Disquisitiones Arithmeticae' at twenty-four, computed where the newly discovered Ceres would reappear, gave least squares a powerful theoretical foundation by linking it to the normal error curve, and worked out results he kept private for years under his motto 'few, but ripe' — including a non-Euclidean geometry whose mathematics of curvature, through Riemann, later gave relativity its language. His name still marks the bell curve, a unit of magnetism, and the shape of curved space.
aliases: The Master of the Imperfect Measurement
The Shadow Over Bolyai and Lobachevsky
indexTerms: Gauss, Carl Friedrich (tr. Arthur A. Clarke)

title: Carthage
summary: The Phoenician trading city that became Rome's most dangerous rival and was completely destroyed — the paradigm case of the losers' civilization in Western historical memory, known almost entirely through the accounts of those who destroyed it.
aliases: Hannibal as Strategic Failure Despite Tactical Brilliance

title: Cassava
summary: A drought-tolerant root domesticated in the Amazon, cassava feeds hundreds of millions of people — especially across Africa — yet the plant is poisonous, and only elaborate traditional processing makes it safe to eat. Its story is a lesson in how accumulated cultural knowledge turns a deadly plant into a staple, and a reminder that the world's most important crops are not always its most famous.
aliases: The Builders of a New Food System
The Late Arrival of Formal Research
indexTerms: Hillocks, R.J., Thresh, J.M., and Bellotti, A.C. (eds.)

title: Caste
summary: One of the world's oldest and most elaborate surviving hierarchies of birth — a system that ranked South Asian people into hereditary, endogamous, occupational groups, placed some communities outside the order altogether as 'untouchable,' and rested on ideas of ritual purity and pollution drawn from Hindu texts. Now illegal in India yet far from dead, caste has structured marriage, labor, worship, and violence for millennia, produced the Dalit liberation movement and B.R. Ambedkar's foundational challenge, and spread with the South Asian diaspora into new legal and political contests abroad.
aliases: The System Seen from the Bottom
The Tradition That Can Heal Itself
The System That Was Also Made Modern

title: Cattle
summary: Domesticated from the wild aurochs roughly ten thousand years ago, cattle became the most versatile animal in human history — supplying meat, milk, leather, dung fuel, and traction power that made plow agriculture possible. As walking stores of wealth across pastoral and agrarian societies worldwide, they shaped economies, religions, and languages. Today, industrialized at a scale of roughly one billion animals, cattle are among the heaviest ecological burdens humanity places on the land.
indexTerms: P. Gerber, H. Steinfeld, B. Henderson et al. (FAO)
Paolo Ajmone-Marsan, Jose Fernando Garcia, and Johannes A. Lenstra

title: Cement
summary: The powdered binder — most importantly Portland cement, made by calcining limestone and clay at extreme heat — that, when mixed with water, undergoes a chemical reaction called hydration and hardens into a stone-like solid. Cement is the active ingredient in concrete, mortar, and most modern masonry. It is the active ingredient in concrete, the most-used manufactured material after water, and its production is one of the largest single sources of human carbon emissions on earth.

title: Central Banking
summary: The institution that sits above the banks — issuer of the national currency, lender of last resort when a panic threatens to bring the system down, and setter of the interest rate that prices borrowing across an economy. The central bank manages the value of money, and its power has grown enormously even as its legitimacy stays contested.
indexTerms: Kydland, Finn E.; Prescott, Edward C.

title: Ceramics
summary: Inorganic materials hardened by fire — above all fired clay — that represent humanity's first synthetic material: an irreversible chemical transformation of earth into something new, running unbroken from the earliest cooking pot to the heat shields of spacecraft.
aliases: The Ancient Technology at the Frontier
indexTerms: Kingery, W. David and Pamela B. Vandiver
W. David Kingery and Pamela B. Vandiver

title: Chaco Canyon
summary: The monumental center of the Ancestral Puebloan world in the high desert of northwestern New Mexico between roughly 850 and 1150 CE — a place of enormous multi-story great houses, precise astronomical alignments, and a network of straight roads, built in a harsh landscape, debated by archaeologists, and held sacred by the Pueblo nations descended from its builders.
aliases: A Center Read From Stone and Wood
A Living Sacred Place, Not a Lost World

title: Champa
summary: A Hindu kingdom of seafarers and temple-builders that endured on the central and southern coast of what is now Vietnam for more than a millennium, its last southern remnant absorbed by Vietnam in 1832. Neither Chinese nor Vietnamese, the Cham took up Indian religion, script, and statecraft, grew rich on the sea route between China and India, and left red-brick temple-towers still standing among the rice fields — yet Champa itself has nearly dropped out of the world's memory.
indexTerms: Trần Kỳ Phương and Bruce M. Lockhart (eds.)

title: Charlemagne
summary: The Frankish king crowned Emperor of the Romans on Christmas Day 800 CE who unified most of Western Europe under a single ruler for the first time since the Roman Empire's fall, promoted literacy and learning in the Carolingian Renaissance, and whose empire — despite fragmenting within a generation of his death — became the template for European political and religious identity that persisted for a thousand years.

title: Charles Darwin
summary: The naturalist who proposed natural selection as the mechanism of evolution, producing a theory so coherent and so well-evidenced that it made divine creation scientifically unnecessary within biology, and permanently transformed how humans understand the relationship between life and time, and established biology as a historical science
indexTerms: Stephen Jay Gould and Niles Eldredge
Massimo Pigliucci and Gerd B. Müller (eds.)

title: Charles Dickens
summary: The English novelist, born in 1812, who became one of the most popular and influential writers of the nineteenth century and made a whole age see itself. Pulled out of school as a boy to work in a blacking factory while his father sat in a debtors' prison, he turned the misery of industrial England into unforgettable stories. He published most of his great novels in cheap monthly or weekly instalments, building vast suspenseful plots read aloud in homes across Britain and beyond: Oliver Twist, A Christmas Carol, David Copperfield, Bleak House, Great Expectations. He fused comedy, sentiment, and biting social criticism, attacking the workhouse, the delays of the law, and industrial squalor, and gave the language a gallery of immortal characters whose name became an adjective. His art also carries shadows. Fagin, the villain of Oliver Twist, is drawn in crude antisemitic stereotype, though Dickens later softened the text and wrote a kindly Jewish character after Jewish readers objected. In 1858 he cast off his wife Catherine, mother of his ten children, in a cold public separation. He died in 1870, mourned across the English-speaking world.
aliases: The husband who cast off his wife

title: Charlie Chaplin
summary: The English actor, director, writer, and composer who, as the bowler-hatted Tramp, became one of the most widely recognized people on earth and turned silent slapstick into a universal art. Born into Dickensian poverty in 1889 London, with a workhouse childhood and a mother lost to mental illness, Chaplin learned his craft in the music hall and then conquered the new medium of film. By the late 1910s the Tramp — cane, baggy trousers, toothbrush moustache, the dignity of the poor — had made him one of the first truly global movie stars, and he co-founded United Artists to control his own work. Films like The Kid, The Gold Rush, City Lights, and Modern Times fused breathtaking physical comedy with genuine pathos and social feeling; he clung to silence long after sound arrived, then spoke in The Great Dictator (1940) to satirize Hitler. His private life drew lasting scandal — a pattern of relationships with very young women, several marriages to teenagers, and a bruising 1940s paternity suit. Branded a communist sympathizer in the Red Scare, Chaplin, a lifelong British subject who never took US citizenship, was effectively barred from re-entering the United States in 1952 and settled in Switzerland. The country that exiled him gave him an honorary Oscar in 1972, to a long standing ovation. He died in 1977.
aliases: The man who proved the new art could be profound
The subversive who was driven out
The pattern the films do not show

title: Che Guevara
summary: The Argentine doctor turned Marxist guerrilla who helped Fidel Castro win the Cuban Revolution, served in its new government and oversaw the revolutionary tribunals and executions at La Cabaña, theorized armed revolution for the whole Third World, died trying to spread it in Bolivia in 1967, and was reborn as one of the most reproduced photographs of the twentieth century — a face that means rebellion to millions, ruthlessness to many others, and money to the companies that sell it.

title: Chichén Itzá
summary: A great pre-Columbian Maya city in the northern Yucatán of Mexico, which rose in the Terminal Classic and dominated much of the northern lowlands between roughly 800 and 1100 CE before declining by about 1200 to 1250. Its monuments include El Castillo (the Temple of Kukulcán), the largest Mesoamerican ball court, the round Caracol, the Temple of the Warriors, and the Sacred Cenote into which offerings were thrown. Famous for a blend of Maya and central-Mexican styles whose origin scholars now debate, it remained a place of pilgrimage after its decline and is today a UNESCO World Heritage Site and a New 7 Wonders of the World.
aliases: The Well That Outlived the City
The Conquest That Explained Too Much
A Node in a Wider Network
The Wonder on the Day Trip
indexTerms: Cynthia Kristan-Graham and Jeff Karl Kowalski
Clemency Coggins and Orrin C. Shane III (eds.)
Michael D. Coe and Stephen Houston
Cynthia Kristan-Graham and Jeff Karl Kowalski (eds.)

title: Chimamanda Ngozi Adichie
summary: The Nigerian novelist and essayist whose Half of a Yellow Sun and Americanah, and whose talks 'The Danger of a Single Story' and 'We Should All Be Feminists,' carried Igbo and Nigerian life into the center of twenty-first-century world literature — a writer who insisted that Africans be granted the full complexity of their own stories, refusing the single story in both its colonial and its sentimental forms.
associatedWorks: The Danger of a Single Story

title: Chinua Achebe
summary: The Nigerian novelist whose Things Fall Apart (1958) became the most influential founding work of modern African literature in English — a writer who insisted that Africans could and should render their own societies from the inside, in full human complexity, and who in doing so permanently changed who held the pen over Africa's past.

title: Cholera
summary: The acute diarrheal disease caused by Vibrio cholerae that spread in seven global pandemics from 1817 to the present, killing millions and driving the sanitary reforms that created modern public health infrastructure. Cholera is the founding disease of epidemiology: John Snow's 1854 investigation of a London outbreak established the field's core methods before germ theory existed, demonstrating that disease patterns could reveal causation through spatial analysis alone.
aliases: Cholera as the Disease of Poverty Made Visible
Cholera as Proof of What Remains Undone

title: Christianity
summary: The religious movement that began as a Jewish sect in Roman Palestine, became the official religion of the Roman Empire, shaped Western civilization's law, art, philosophy, and political thought for two thousand years, and now claims more than two billion adherents worldwide — the largest and most geographically diverse religion in history.

title: Cicero
summary: The Roman orator, lawyer, politician, and philosopher who served as consul in 63 BCE, wrote the foundational Latin works on rhetoric, political philosophy, and moral philosophy, and was executed in 43 BCE on Mark Antony's orders — leaving a body of work that was rediscovered in the 14th century by Petrarch and became the intellectual foundation of Renaissance humanism, early modern republicanism, and the Western rhetorical tradition.

title: Citizenship
summary: Citizenship is the political technology that divides humanity into members and non-members of a state. It grants rights to some and withholds them from others. Understanding citizenship requires understanding not just who belongs but why the boundary exists, what it costs those outside it, and who has historically been expelled from the category.

title: Civil Service Examinations
summary: The Chinese civil-service examination system, the keju, was the longest-running attempt in history to staff a government by tested learning rather than by birth. Maturing under the Sui and Tang around the seventh century and elaborated under the Song, it required candidates to master the Confucian classics and write to a strict form, advancing through sealed local, provincial, and palace examinations toward the jinshi degree that opened the door to office. Over roughly thirteen centuries it produced a scholar-official class and a durable bureaucracy, offered a narrow but real route upward for talented commoners, and drew admiration from Enlightenment Europe before its abolition in 1905. Its costs — rote learning, the advantage of the wealthy who could afford to study, and a conservatism critics later blamed for stifling change — are as much a part of the record as its achievements.
aliases: The Machine That Made an Elite

title: Claude Monet
summary: The French painter (1840–1926) who became the central figure of Impressionism, made working outdoors in changing light central to his art, while still building his canvases with deliberation, and spent his life trying to record not objects but the fleeting effects of sunlight and atmosphere. His painting Impression, Sunrise gave the movement its name, taken from a hostile critic's mockery. Through his series of haystacks, poplars, and Rouen Cathedral, and the vast Water Lilies of his garden at Giverny, he is widely seen as one of the painters who broke the long grip of realistic representation and helped open the door to modern art.
aliases: The Light of a Modernizing France

title: Clay
summary: The fine-grained, plastic sediment — shaped when wet, rigid when dried, fused when fired — that served as humanity's first durable fired vessel, first building block, and first writing surface, and whose abundance in river valleys made it the quiet substrate of the earliest cities, the oldest libraries, and the potter's art.
aliases: The Archive That Survived by Accident

title: Cleopatra VII
summary: The last ruler of Ptolemaic Egypt — Greek by dynasty, Egyptian by cultural identity and political strategy — who allied with Julius Caesar and Mark Antony in the dying decades of the Roman Republic, presided over Egypt's absorption into the Roman Empire, and became one of the most mythologized figures in Western history, her actual political genius largely obscured by two thousand years of sexual legend.

title: Climate Change
summary: The ongoing warming of the Earth's climate system driven by human greenhouse gas emissions — the defining environmental force of the present and future
indexTerms: Global Commission on the Economy and Climate

title: Coal
summary: The fossilized sunlight that powered the Industrial Revolution — by providing the concentrated energy that broke the organic economy's land constraint, created the working class, built the railway network, and began accumulating the atmospheric carbon that is now reshaping the planet's climate
indexTerms: Astrid Kander, Paolo Malanima, Paul Warde

title: Coffee
summary: The roasted seed of a tropical shrub that became one of the world's great traded commodities and, more importantly, the social fuel of modern public life — coffee gave the Enlightenment world a sober, stimulating space to gather, talk, and do business, while binding tropical growers to distant consumers through centuries of colonial agriculture and price volatility.
indexTerms: Topik, Steven and Clarence-Smith, William Gervase (eds.)
Steven Topik and William Gervase Clarence-Smith (eds.)

title: Colonialism
summary: The establishment and maintenance of political and economic control by one society over another — the system through which European powers between the 15th and 20th centuries dominated most of the world's surface, extracting resources, displacing populations, restructuring economies, and imposing administrative systems whose consequences persist long after formal colonial rule ended.
aliases: Agency, Hybridity, and the Limits of the Archive
indexTerms: Frederick Cooper and Ann Laura Stoler (eds.)
Daron Acemoglu, Simon Johnson, and James A. Robinson

title: Common Law
summary: A legal system in which much of the law is made by judges deciding individual cases rather than only by lawmakers writing codes. Its engine is precedent (stare decisis): a court's ruling on a point of law binds later courts in similar cases, so the law grows case by case out of accumulated decisions. Born in England after 1066 and carried across the world by the British Empire, the common law today governs the legal systems of the United States, Canada, Australia, India, and much of Africa and the Caribbean — often imposed on colonized peoples over their own traditions. It is one of the two major Western-derived legal families of the modern world, set against the civil-law systems of Europe and Latin America that build the law from comprehensive codes.
aliases: The law that arrived with the ships
indexTerms: Maitland, Frederic William and Pollock, Frederick

title: Communism
summary: The political and economic theory holding that private ownership of the means of production should be abolished and replaced by common ownership, with the goal of a classless society — and the name for the revolutionary movements and states that have attempted to build it.

title: Computing
summary: The infrastructure for processing and transforming information at scale — from Babbage's mechanical difference engine through the first electronic computers to the networked systems that now mediate commerce, communication, science, and government worldwide. Computing is the story of how humanity built infrastructure that amplifies information processing as roads amplify movement and electricity amplifies energy.

title: Concrete
summary: The composite building material — a mixture of aggregate, water, and a binding agent — that has been the primary construction medium of every major civilization since Rome, and whose modern form, reinforced with steel, is the material substrate of the contemporary built world: its dams, bridges, highways, apartment blocks, and infrastructure.

title: Confucianism
summary: The ethical and social tradition rooted in the teachings of Confucius that became the moral grammar, statecraft, and family ethic of China and much of East Asia -- made the doctrine of one of history’s longest-running examination-based bureaucracies, attacked in the 20th century as the dead hand of the past, and invoked again today.

title: Confucius
summary: The Chinese philosopher whose teachings on moral cultivation, proper relationships, and the virtuous ruler became the intellectual foundation of East Asian political culture for two and a half millennia — and whose ideas have never stopped being contested, weaponized, and reinterpreted
indexTerms: E. Bruce Brooks and A. Taeko Brooks

title: Constantinople
summary: The city founded by Constantine in 330 CE on the strait between Europe and Asia that served as the capital of the Roman Empire, then the Byzantine Empire for over a millennium, before falling to the Ottoman Turks in 1453 and becoming Istanbul — a transformation that marked the end of the medieval world.
aliases: The Catastrophe That Remade the West

title: Constitution
summary: A constitution is a technology for binding power — a set of rules that today’s government must follow because yesterday’s decisions made them binding. The central puzzle is how this can work at all: if the sovereign is supreme, who enforces the limits on the sovereign?
aliases: The constitution as law because it was made and enacted, not because it is just
indexTerms: Alexander Hamilton, James Madison, John Jay
Mark Graber, Sanford Levinson, and Mark Tushnet (eds.)

title: Copper
summary: The first metal worked by human hands — soft enough to shape without smelting, abundant enough to spread across cultures, and the base element of bronze — whose adoption reorganized human labor, warfare, and trade across the ancient world and whose descendants still wire the electrical infrastructure of the modern one.

title: Coral Reefs
summary: A coral reef is a vast living structure built over thousands of years by colonies of tiny animals whose limestone skeletons accumulate into the most biodiverse habitat in the sea — a system that covers a tiny fraction of the ocean floor yet shelters perhaps a quarter of all marine species, shields coastlines, and feeds hundreds of millions, and that warming and acidifying seas have now made one of the most threatened ecosystems on Earth.
aliases: The reef as the ground of survival
The reef as protection and livelihood
The reef as indicator and archive

title: Cordoba
summary: The city on the Guadalquivir in southern Spain that rose from a Roman provincial capital to become, as Qurtuba, the capital of Muslim al-Andalus and one of the largest and most learned cities of the tenth-century world — whose Great Mosque, later turned into a cathedral, still stands as the central monument and the central argument about who the city belongs to.
aliases: The Lost Capital of the West
indexTerms: Heather Ecker and others (Metropolitan Museum of Art)

title: Cotton
summary: The fiber that clothed the ancient world and reorganized the modern one — cotton's journey from domesticated plant to global commodity drove the transatlantic slave trade, industrialization in Britain, and the political economy of empire, making it the material thread connecting the most consequential transformations of the past five centuries.
indexTerms: Federal Writers' Project, U.S. Works Progress Administration

title: Cozumel
summary: An island off the Caribbean coast of Mexico's Yucatan Peninsula that was one of the most important pilgrimage destinations in the Maya world, especially in the Postclassic period (roughly 1200–1500 CE). Maya travelers, by Spanish accounts often women hoping for safe childbirth, crossed the sea to consult an oracle of the goddess often identified with Ix Chel, associated with the moon, healing, childbirth, weaving, and divination. Cortes stopped here in 1519 at the start of the conquest of Mexico; disease and conquest later emptied the island. Today it is one of the world's busiest cruise-ship ports and a famous scuba-diving destination on the Mesoamerican Reef.
aliases: The Shrine as Idolatry to Be Suppressed
The Reef Island and Its Curated Past
indexTerms: David A. Freidel and Jeremy A. Sabloff
Michael D. Coe and Stephen Houston

title: Credit
summary: The extension of purchasing power based on trust in a future payment — the claim side of the debt relationship and the social mechanism through which promises to pay have been made tradeable, circulated as near-money, and used to build everything from Mesopotamian grain networks to global capital markets.
indexTerms: Bank of England (Michael McLeay, Amar Radia, Ryland Thomas)

title: Cricket
summary: Cricket is a bat-and-ball game between two teams of eleven, codified in eighteenth-century England and carried across the British Empire as a tool of imperial culture meant to teach colonial subjects an idealized set of 'English' values. The colonized embraced it, mastered it, and turned it around: beating England at its own game became a form of dignity and self-assertion. Today the sport's heartland is the Indian subcontinent, where it is a near-religion, India-Pakistan matches draw hundreds of millions, and the money and power have shifted decisively from London to India. Cricket is the rare imperial inheritance the former subjects made more their own than the rulers ever were.
aliases: Cricket as the Theatre of Empire
Cricket as a Game That Was Always Ours

title: Cusco
summary: The capital and sacred center of the Inca empire, high in the Peruvian Andes — a planned city the Incas called the navel of the world, rebuilt in monumental stone in the fifteenth century, seized by the Spanish in 1533 and overbuilt with churches and mansions raised directly on its Inca foundations.
aliases: Building the New Faith on the Old
The Stones That Outlasted the Conquest
Reading the City Without Its Own Words

title: Customs Houses
summary: The institution through which states have taxed, inspected, and controlled the movement of goods across borders and through ports — generating revenue, asserting sovereignty, regulating trade, and producing the bureaucratic frontier at which commerce and state power meet.
indexTerms: Findlay, Ronald and O'Rourke, Kevin H.

title: Cyclones
summary: A cyclone is what a tropical cyclone is called in the Indian Ocean and Bay of Bengal — the same storm that goes by 'hurricane' in the Atlantic and 'typhoon' in the Pacific. The physics is identical, but the geography is not. The Bay of Bengal is shallow and funnel-shaped, the delta at its head is one of the flattest and most crowded places on earth, and the storm surge has nowhere to go but over the land. The result: the deadliest tropical cyclones in human history have struck here, and the human story of South Asian cyclones is one of catastrophic mass death followed, in recent decades, by a genuine and remarkable survival revolution.
aliases: The storm that came in the night
The storm the government would not let anyone fix

title: Cyrus the Great
summary: The Persian king who, in roughly two decades, rose from a subject people to overthrow the Medes, Lydians, and Babylonians and assembled the Achaemenid Empire — the largest the world had yet seen — governing it through a pragmatic policy of restoring local cults and ruling through local elites that the Hebrew Bible praised and that a clay cylinder recorded, a record later, and contestedly, called the first charter of human rights.
aliases: Cyrus the Model Prince and the Defeated King

title: Dahomey
summary: The Kingdom of Dahomey (c. 1600–1894) was a tightly centralized West African state of the Fon people, ruled from Abomey in present-day Benin. It built a standing army that included the all-female Agojie regiments, grew rich and then dependent on supplying captives to the Atlantic slave trade through the port of Ouidah, and fell to French conquest in the 1890s. The entry asks what kind of state Dahomey was, holding its real organizational power and its role in the slave trade together rather than choosing between them.
aliases: The kingdom from the wrong end of its wars
Dahomey as a business partner and a story to tell
An inheritance that is proud and painful at once

title: Damascus
summary: An oasis city watered by the Barada river and among the oldest continuously inhabited places on earth — ruled in turn by Aramaeans, Assyrians, Persians, Greeks, and Romans, made the capital of the Umayyad Caliphate that ruled from Spain to India, and today the contested capital of Syria.

title: Dante Alighieri
summary: The Florentine poet (1265–1321) who, banished from his city for life under threat of being burned alive if he returned, wrote one of the greatest poems of the European Middle Ages in a highly crafted Tuscan vernacular rather than the Latin of the learned. In choosing the vernacular for the highest subject matter, and in making that poem the cornerstone of a literary tradition, he did more than any other early writer to make Tuscan the model for literary Italian, and he is held by tradition to be the father of the Italian language and one of the supreme poets of the Western canon.

title: Daoism
summary: China's great philosophy of letting go — the tradition of the Dao, the Way, which set the spontaneity of nature against the rules of society, taught that the wisest action is often no action, and grew from a handful of cryptic texts into an organized religion of gods, alchemists, and seekers of immortality.

title: Darius the Great
summary: The Achaemenid king who seized the Persian throne in 522 BCE amid a violent succession crisis and then reorganized the largest empire the world had yet seen -- dividing it into provinces with fixed tribute, standardizing coinage, building the Royal Road and the ceremonial capital at Persepolis -- while leaving a cliff-carved inscription that is both his official self-defense and a key that later unlocked cuneiform.
aliases: Darius as the Despot Defeated at Marathon
Darius as Restorer of Order under Ahura Mazda
Darius as Possible Usurper behind the Official Story

title: David Hume
summary: The Scottish philosopher who pushed Enlightenment empiricism to its radical conclusions — showing that reason alone cannot prove causation, establish the self, or justify belief in miracles — and who produced the most systematic challenge to religious belief and rationalist philosophy that the Western tradition has faced.

title: Debt
summary: The social relationship in which one party owes something to another — money, labor, goods, or obligation — and the institution through which that relationship has organized power, enforced hierarchy, funded states, and structured the experience of most people who have ever lived.
indexTerms: Reinhart, Carmen M.; Rogoff, Kenneth S.

title: Decolonization
summary: The process by which European colonial empires dissolved between 1945 and 1975 — as Asian and African peoples won independence through armed struggle, negotiation, and political pressure — transforming the map of the world, creating dozens of new states, and generating the political and economic structures of the contemporary Global South.
indexTerms: Acemoglu, Daron; Johnson, Simon; Robinson, James A.
Jan C. Jansen and Jurgen Osterhammel

title: Democracy
summary: The concept that political authority derives from the governed — and the site of the most active contemporary political contestation

title: Deng Xiaoping
summary: The pragmatic survivor who became China's paramount leader after Mao Zedong's death — twice purged and twice restored — and who, without serving as party chairman, general secretary, or state president, reversed much of Maoist economic policy, launched the 'reform and opening' that built a market economy under one-party rule, lifted hundreds of millions out of poverty in a single generation, and, as paramount leader, backed the decision to send the army against the 1989 Tiananmen Square protests, a crackdown that remains heavily censored inside China.
aliases: The Man Who Sent the Tanks
indexTerms: Nathan, Andrew J., and Perry Link (eds.)

title: Desmond Tutu
summary: The South African Anglican archbishop who became the moral voice of the struggle against apartheid and then the conscience of the democracy that followed — a Nobel Peace laureate who named apartheid a sin and a heresy, gave the world the image of the 'Rainbow Nation,' chaired the Truth and Reconciliation Commission's contested attempt to trade truth for amnesty, and never stopped speaking uncomfortable truths to power, Black and white alike, to the end of his life.
associatedWorks: God Has a Dream: A Vision of Hope for Our Time

title: Diaspora
summary: The dispersion of a people from their original homeland, maintaining collective identity across generations in a foreign land — a concept originating with the Jewish exile in Babylon, extended to the African diaspora produced by the slave trade, and now applied to dozens of dispersed communities worldwide, raising fundamental questions about the relationship between identity, territory, and belonging.
indexTerms: Mintz, Sidney W., and Richard Price

title: Diego Rivera and Mexican Muralism
summary: The movement, with Diego Rivera as its most internationally famous figure, in which the Mexican state turned the walls of its public buildings over to painters who covered them with a vast, fierce epic of Mexican history -- from the Aztec past through the Spanish conquest to the promise of revolution -- making art a weapon of the people and giving a wounded nation a new image of itself.
aliases: Art as a Weapon of the People
indexTerms: Alejandro Anreus, Leonard Folgarait, and Robin Adele Greeley (eds.)

title: Djenné
summary: A trading city on the inland delta of the Niger River in Mali, twin to nearby Djenné-Djenno, one of the oldest known cities in sub-Saharan Africa. Djenné grew rich linking the goldfields of the south to the salt and desert routes of the north, became a respected center of Islamic learning, and is best known today for its Great Mosque, the largest mud-brick building in the world, replastered each year by the whole community.
aliases: The Southern Anchor of the Desert Trade
A City That Grew on Its Own
The Building That Must Be Remade

title: Dmitri Mendeleev
summary: The Russian chemist who, in 1869, arranged the sixty-odd known elements by atomic weight into a single table whose recurring pattern he called the periodic law — and who set himself apart from others circling the same idea by leaving gaps for undiscovered elements and predicting their properties in detail. When gallium, scandium, and germanium were found within fifteen years, matching his forecasts with startling precision, the periodic table became the organizing map of chemistry. He worked without knowing why the pattern held; the deeper basis, atomic number, came only later through Moseley. Element 101, mendelevium, bears his name, though he never won the Nobel Prize.
aliases: The Author of the Periodic Law
associatedWorks: On the Relationship of the Properties of the Elements to their Atomic Weights
The Natural System of the Elements

title: DNA
summary: The molecule that carries genetic information in living organisms — deoxyribonucleic acid, whose double-helix structure was determined in 1953 by Watson, Crick, Franklin, and Wilkins — and the conceptual foundation that unified biology, explained heredity, enabled genetic medicine, and transformed humanity's relationship to its own biology.
indexTerms: Doudna, Jennifer A. and Sternberg, Samuel H.
Supreme Court of the United States

title: Don Quixote
summary: Miguel de Cervantes’s two-part novel (1605, 1615) about a Spanish gentleman who reads so many chivalric romances that he loses his mind and sets out to become a knight-errant — widely regarded as the first modern novel, a founding text of European literature, and an inexhaustible meditation on the relationship between imagination and reality.
aliases: The Ingenious Gentleman Don Quixote of La Mancha
El ingenioso hidalgo don Quijote de la Mancha
The Foundation of the Spanish Genius

title: Dream of the Red Chamber
summary: The mid-18th-century Chinese novel by Cao Xueqin, also called The Story of the Stone, widely regarded as the greatest work of Chinese fiction and one of the Four Great Classical Novels of China.
