summary: The 1066 invasion in which William, Duke of Normandy, seized the English throne, defeating and killing King Harold at the Battle of Hastings and being crowned on Christmas Day as William the Conqueror. It was the last successful foreign conquest of England, and it remade the country's ruling class, language, law, and landscape so completely that the result is still audible in modern English. The conquest was brutal, marked by the devastating Harrying of the North, and it left behind both a fused English language and the lasting myth of a free Anglo-Saxon age crushed by a Norman yoke.
aliases: The State That Outlived Its Rulers

title: The Novel
summary: The extended prose fiction form that emerged in Europe in the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries and became the dominant literary form of the modern world — distinguished from earlier narrative forms by its length, its focus on individual psychological interiority, its engagement with social reality, and its address to a reading public rather than a listening audience. The novel is not merely a literary form but an epistemological one: it develops the capacity to inhabit other minds and imagine other lives.
aliases: The Novel as Global Form and Cultural Imposition
The Novel as Women's Form and Women's Constraint
indexTerms: Raymond A. Mar, Keith Oatley, et al.

title: The Odyssey
summary: An ancient Greek epic poem attributed to Homer, composed around the 8th century BCE in the tradition of oral poetry, following Odysseus on his ten-year journey home after the Trojan War -- a foundational work of Western literature whose narrative templates have been reworked in every subsequent century.
aliases: Inheriting the Canon from the Outside

title: The Olmec
summary: The earliest major civilization of Mesoamerica, centered on the hot, wet lowlands of the Gulf Coast of Mexico from roughly 1500 to 400 BCE. The Olmec built the first large ceremonial centers in the region, first at San Lorenzo and later at La Venta, and carved colossal basalt heads weighing many tons from stone hauled across long distances. They worked jade with great skill and left behind a recurring set of religious images, including a part-human, part-jaguar being. Whether they were the single "mother culture" that shaped everything that followed in Mesoamerica, or one of several early "sister cultures," is still debated.
aliases: The Olmec as the Root of Mesoamerica
What the Stone Will and Won't Tell
indexTerms: Michael D. Coe and Rex Koontz
Jeffrey P. Blomster, Hector Neff, and Michael D. Glascock
Mary E. D. Pohl, Kevin O. Pope, and Christopher von Nagy
Rodríguez Martínez, María del Carmen; Ortíz Ceballos, Ponciano; Coe, Michael D.; et al.
Jill Guthrie and Elizabeth P. Benson

title: The Opium Wars
summary: Two conflicts between Britain and China (1839–1842 and 1856–1860) fought over British insistence on the right to sell opium in China and China's attempts to prohibit the trade. The Opium Wars inaugurated China's 'century of humiliation,' opened Chinese markets and territory to foreign powers through unequal treaties, and remain defining events in Chinese national memory and foreign policy.
aliases: The Century of Humiliation Begins Here
Opium as Colonial Extraction from Both Ends

title: The Ottoman Empire
summary: The Turkic Muslim empire that ruled Anatolia, the Middle East, North Africa, and southeastern Europe for six centuries — the longest-lasting Islamic empire, the inheritor of Byzantium, the context for modern Turkey and much of the contemporary Middle East, and a civilization whose complexity Western historical writing has systematically underestimated.

title: The Ottoman Tanzimat Reforms
summary: A program of sweeping state reform in the Ottoman Empire between 1839 and 1876, aimed at centralizing the government, modernizing the army, law, taxation, and schooling, and binding a vast multi-religious population together by promising every subject equal protection. Opened by the Gulhane Edict of 1839 and extended by the 1856 Reform Edict, the Tanzimat reshaped the Ottoman state, unsettled the old Muslim-dominant order, and ended in the empire's first constitution in 1876.
aliases: The Loss of a Sanctioned Order
indexTerms: Stanford J. Shaw and Ezel Kural Shaw

title: The Pala Empire
summary: The Buddhist dynasty of Bengal and Bihar that, as Buddhism faded across the rest of India, became its last great imperial patron — sustaining Nalanda, founding Vikramashila, and serving as the launch point from which Vajrayana Buddhism passed to Tibet.
indexTerms: Taranatha (Lama Chimpa and Alaka Chattopadhyaya, trans.)
Abhishek Singh Amar and others (Jason Neelis, ed. context)

title: The Paris Commune
summary: The seventy-two days in the spring of 1871 when the working people of Paris seized their own city and governed it themselves as a radical, democratic commune, before the French national government retook the capital by force and drowned the rising in blood. After France lost a humiliating war to Prussia and Paris endured a long siege, the city rose against a conservative government that tried to disarm it. For about two months the Commune separated church and state, made education free and secular, handed idle workshops to worker cooperatives, capped officials' salaries, and flew the red flag. In the 'Bloody Week' of late May the army crushed it, killing many thousands of Parisians in combat and summary executions — older estimates often range from 10,000 to 20,000, though some recent work argues lower; in its final days the Commune itself shot hostages, including the Archbishop of Paris. The brief experiment became the founding myth of modern revolutionary socialism.
aliases: The View from the Revolutionary Left

title: The Parliament
summary: The representative assembly through which a society makes its laws, decides its taxes, controls its spending, and holds its rulers to account through open debate and recorded votes — an institution that grew out of medieval councils summoned to grant a ruler money, won the long struggle over the power of the purse, shifted from advising a monarch to governing in his place, and then spread across the world as the working machinery of representative government, carrying both its promise of consent and its persistent failures of inclusion.
aliases: The Parliament as the Growth of Consent
The Parliament as One Case Among Many

title: The Partition of India
summary: The division of British India in August 1947 into the independent states of India and Pakistan — accompanied by the largest mass migration in human history, between one and two million deaths, and the displacement of fifteen million people — that shaped the subcontinent's politics for generations.

title: The Patent
summary: A temporary, state-granted monopoly over an invention, given in exchange for public disclosure of how it works — a deliberate bargain between the inventor's reward and the public's freedom to use knowledge, and one of the most contested instruments of innovation policy.
aliases: The Other Side of the Bargain
indexTerms: George Selgin and John L. Turner

title: The Persian Empire
summary: The Achaemenid Persian Empire — founded by Cyrus the Great around 550 BCE and destroyed by Alexander the Great in 330 BCE — was the largest empire the ancient world had yet seen, stretching from the Aegean to the Indus, governing perhaps 40 percent of the world's population through a system of administrative tolerance, royal roads, and satrapal governance that became the template for empire across the ancient world.

title: The Philippines Under Spain
summary: The 333-year period during which Spain ruled an archipelago of more than seven thousand islands in Southeast Asia — governing it from Spanish Mexico rather than Europe, knitting it to the world through a single galleon a year between Manila and Acapulco, and converting most of its lowland peoples to Roman Catholicism through the friar orders, who held vast estates and often more power than the civil government, while the Muslim south and the highlands were never fully subdued and resistance ran from Lapulapu to the revolution that ended Spanish rule.
aliases: The South That Was Never Taken

title: The Phoenicians
summary: The Phoenicians were the Semitic-speaking Canaanite city-states of the Levantine coast — chiefly Tyre, Sidon, Byblos, and Arwad — who flourished from roughly 1200 to 300 BCE, especially in the centuries after the Bronze Age Collapse. They were the master maritime traders and shipbuilders of the Mediterranean, planting trading posts and colonies from Cyprus to the Atlantic, the greatest of them Carthage. They were never a unified empire but a network of independent cities, usually under the overlordship of Assyria, then Babylon, then Persia, whose navy they supplied. Their consonantal script reshaped writing across the Mediterranean, and their purple dye gave them their outside name.

title: The Pillow Book
summary: The early-eleventh-century miscellany by Sei Shonagon, a lady-in-waiting to the empress Teishi at the Heian court of Japan. Not a story but a 'following-the-brush' notebook (zuihitsu) of lists, opinions, court anecdotes, and nature notes, it records exactly what charmed and disgusted its author with dazzling wit and snobbery. Its governing mood is okashi, the bright delight in what is clever and pretty, a counterweight to the melancholy of The Tale of Genji. Composed over years around the year 1000, it survives in several differently ordered manuscript versions, and the origin of its title remains uncertain.
aliases: The Pillow Book of Sei Shonagon
The Founding of Following the Brush
The Bright Half of the Heian Mood

title: The Plantation
summary: The agricultural-industrial system that organized enslaved and coerced labor to mass-produce cash crops — sugar, tobacco, cotton, coffee — for distant markets. The plantation was not a farm but a factory in the field: a regime of gang labor, surveillance, and violence that ran on a precise calendar, generated immense wealth for owners and the economies they fed, built racially ordered slave societies across the Atlantic world, and left long afterlives in sharecropping, the convict-lease farm, and the company estate.
aliases: The Plantation as Engine of the Modern World
indexTerms: Alan L. Olmstead and Paul W. Rhode

title: The Plow
summary: A blade dragged through soil to break and turn it for planting — the plow expanded large-scale grain agriculture — making more soils farmable and more land workable with animal traction — and in doing so helped shape property, labor, gender roles, and the landscapes of the farming world, from the scratch-plow of the ancient Near East to the steel moldboard that stripped the American prairie and helped feed the Dust Bowl.
aliases: The Tool That Broke the Sod
indexTerms: Alberto Alesina, Paola Giuliano, and Nathan Nunn

title: The Port
summary: The institution through which maritime commerce, migration, disease, and ideas moved between worlds — a physical node of docks and warehouses, a regulatory space of customs and quarantine, a cosmopolitan contact zone where strangers met on commercial terms, and a labor system whose containerization revolution in the mid-20th century remade global trade and displaced the dockworker communities that had defined port cities for centuries.

title: The Post Office
summary: The state-run network that carries written messages and parcels to every address at a uniform price — the first communication infrastructure to bind a whole territory together. Built on ancient royal courier relays, transformed by Rowland Hill's Penny Post of 1840 and the prepaid postage stamp, the postal system created a national reading public, funded mail-order commerce, pioneered the postal money order and savings bank, and gave rise to the address system itself. It was also a tool of the state: officials read the mail long before privacy law protected it. The template every later communication network was measured against.
aliases: The Post as Instrument of Government
The Post as the Infrastructure of Modern Social Life
indexTerms: Winseck, Dwayne R. and Robert M. Pike

title: The Power Plant
summary: The central facility where fuel or flowing water is converted into electricity at industrial scale — the place where the abstract idea of 'energy policy' becomes concrete, where a building full of spinning turbines whose fuel choice, multiplied across a grid, decides how much carbon a civilization emits.
aliases: The Power Plant as Physical System
The Power Plant as Institutional Choice
The Power Plant as the Carbon Decision

title: The Prison
summary: The modern institution that punishes by depriving people of liberty over measured time — a relatively recent invention that replaced the public, bodily punishments of earlier eras with confinement behind walls. The prison promised humane, rational reform through isolation, labor, and surveillance, and it grew instead into a vast machinery of confinement whose results are deeply contested, reaching its most extreme form in the mass incarceration of the late-twentieth-century United States, with its sharp racial dimension.

title: The Progressive Era
summary: The Progressive Era (roughly the 1890s to about 1920) was the burst of American reform that tried to tame the new industrial age. Alarmed by giant corporations, corrupt city machines, and brutal working conditions, reformers turned to government and expertise to regulate, clean up, and humanize their society — winning trust-busting, the income tax, food and drug laws, the direct election of senators, Prohibition, and votes for women. The same movement also embraced racial segregation and eugenics and largely excluded Black Americans, which is why its legacy remains genuinely contested.
aliases: The era as the rescue of an industrial society
The era from the people it excluded
The era as the contested birth of the modern state

title: The Protestant Reformation
summary: The 16th-century religious movement that fractured Western Christianity, produced the modern state system, and demonstrated what the printing press could do

title: The Prussian State
summary: The small, sandy north-German kingdom that, through relentless discipline, an outsized army, and a famously efficient bureaucracy, punched far above its weight, forged modern Germany under its rule, and gave the world both the model of the capable state and the word "Prussian" as a byword for militarism.
aliases: Prussia as the root of militarism
Prussia as a betrayed code of duty

title: The Pump
summary: A device that moves fluid against gravity or pressure — it drained the mines, watered the fields and cities, and its desperate struggle against floodwater in deep coal and tin mines directly triggered the invention of the steam engine, making the pump the problem that launched the Industrial Revolution.

title: The Pyramids of Giza
summary: Three great stone pyramids on the Giza plateau outside modern Cairo, built during Egypt's Fourth Dynasty in the Old Kingdom, roughly 2600 to 2500 BCE. The largest, the Great Pyramid of Khufu, rose about 146 metres and was the tallest human-made structure on earth for nearly four thousand years. With the pyramids of Khafre and Menkaure, the Great Sphinx, queens' pyramids, and mortuary temples, they formed royal burial complexes meant to turn a dead king into a god. Built by a large organized workforce, not by enslaved Israelites or whip-driven slaves, they are the only surviving Wonder of the ancient world and the global emblem of Egypt.

title: The Qin Dynasty
summary: The dynasty that lasted only fifteen years and made China — a short, ferocious regime that ended five centuries of warfare, welded a vast field of rival states into the first unified Chinese empire, standardized the written word and the width of cart axles alike, and burned so hot it consumed itself in a generation, leaving a centralized imperial template that shaped China for two thousand years.
aliases: The View from the Swallowed Kingdoms
indexTerms: Yuri Pines, Gideon Shelach, Lothar von Falkenhausen, Robin D.S. Yates (eds.)
Michael Loewe and Edward L. Shaughnessy (eds.)

title: The Qing Dynasty
summary: The last imperial dynasty of China, founded by the Manchu Aisin Gioro clan in 1644 and ruling for 268 years until its collapse in 1912. At its height, the Qing Empire was the largest state in Chinese history and among the most populous on Earth, controlling a territory that included present-day China, Mongolia, Tibet, and Xinjiang — a multiethnic empire whose legacies of conquest, assimilation, and frontier management shape contemporary China.
aliases: The Qing as Patron, Then Conqueror, Then Absent
The Qing as Chinese Dynasty, Not Manchu Empire

title: The Quran
summary: The central religious text of Islam, believed by Muslims to be the direct word of God revealed to the Prophet Muhammad over approximately twenty-three years. The Quran is the foundation of Islamic theology, law, and ethics; the model for classical Arabic prose and poetry; the text memorized in its entirety by millions of hafiz; and the constitution of Islamic civilization in a sense that goes beyond any analogy to scripture in other traditions. Its world effect operates through law, language, ritual, architecture, and political authority across 1,400 years.
aliases: The Quran as Late Antique Text

title: The Ramayana
summary: One of the two great epics of India, a living sacred story for hundreds of millions and a cultural foundation across South and Southeast Asia: the story of the exiled prince Rama, the abduction of his wife Sita by the demon-king Ravana, and the war fought to win her back. Traditionally attributed to the sage-poet Valmiki in Sanskrit, the Ramayana is far more than one text. It exists in hundreds of tellings across languages, religions, and regions of South and Southeast Asia, which differ in their values and even in whose side they take. Its hero Rama is worshipped as an avatar of the god Vishnu, and the epic has been told, sung, painted, and performed for more than two thousand years.
aliases: The Story of the Lord Rama
The Story Told from the Margins

title: The Rebellion of 1857
summary: The vast uprising across northern India against the rule of the British East India Company, which began in May 1857 as a mutiny of the Company's Indian soldiers and swelled into a broad revolt of soldiers, peasants, and dispossessed elites. It was the bloodiest challenge the British ever faced in India, marked by atrocities on both sides, and the Company crushed it with great ferocity. Its defeat ended Company rule, transferred India to the direct government of the British Crown, ended the Mughal dynasty, and left a memory so contested that the event has no single agreed name.

title: The Reconquista
summary: The long, intermittent expansion of Christian kingdoms across the Iberian Peninsula at the expense of Muslim-ruled al-Andalus, conventionally dated 711 to 1492 — a process whose unity as a single, continuous religious 'reconquest' is largely a later construction, since medieval Iberian rulers routinely crossed religious lines, allied with the other faith, and fought their co-religionists. It ended with the fall of Granada in 1492, the same year as the expulsion of Spain's Jews and the first voyage of Columbus.

title: The Reconstruction Amendments
summary: The Thirteenth, Fourteenth, and Fifteenth Amendments to the United States Constitution, ratified between 1865 and 1870 in the years after the Civil War. Together they abolished slavery, made persons born or naturalized in the United States, and subject to its jurisdiction, citizens entitled to due process and the equal protection of the laws, and barred denying the vote on the basis of race. They turned the federal government from a distant power into the guarantor of rights against the states, rewriting what the Constitution was for. Almost at once the Supreme Court read them narrowly and white-supremacist states defied them, leaving the promise unenforced for nearly a century. The civil rights revolution of the mid-twentieth century revived them, and they are now seen as a second founding, the constitutional core of American equality.
aliases: Words Without Force, Then Force Without New Words
indexTerms: United States Supreme Court (Henry Billings Brown)

title: The Renaissance
summary: The cultural and intellectual movement that transformed European art, literature, philosophy, and science between roughly 1300 and 1600 -- centered in Italian city-states, organized through patronage, and driven by the recovery and celebration of ancient Greek and Roman learning.

title: The Republic
summary: Plato's extended philosophical dialogue on justice, the ideal state, and the nature of the soul — the foundational text of Western political philosophy and one of the most influential works in the history of ideas.
indexTerms: Plato (G.M.A. Grube, rev. C.D.C. Reeve, trans.)
Plato (trans. G.M.A. Grube, revised C.D.C. Reeve)

title: The Reservation System
summary: The framework of bounded, 'reserved' lands to which the United States confined Indigenous nations through treaties and federal power. Built as an instrument of dispossession, control, and forced assimilation, it cleared land for settlers and tried to dissolve Native nations through allotment, boarding schools, and agency rule. Yet the same reserved lands and the treaties that created them became the territorial and legal ground on which tribal sovereignty survived. Reservations are not relics: they are the living jurisdictions of self-governing Native nations today.
aliases: Reserved Land as the Ground of Nationhood
The Reservation as Transition to Disappearance
A System Bound by Its Own Legal Forms
indexTerms: David E. Wilkins and K. Tsianina Lomawaima
Vine Deloria Jr. and Clifford M. Lytle

title: The Revolutions of 1848
summary: The wave of revolutions that swept across Europe in 1848, when peoples rose almost everywhere demanding constitutions, nations, and rights, briefly toppled kings and chancellors, and then, within months, saw nearly every revolution crushed and the old order restored. Beginning with risings in Sicily and then Paris in early 1848 and spreading like wildfire to the German states, the Austrian (Habsburg) Empire, the Italian lands, and beyond, the 'Springtime of Peoples' forced rulers to flee or grant concessions, convened parliaments, and lifted censorship. Driven by economic crisis and the ideals of liberalism and nationalism, it looked for a few months as though monarchical Europe might fall. By 1849 the monarchies had recovered their armies and crushed the risings one by one, and the kings came back. Yet the defeated demands of 1848 abolished serfdom in the Habsburg lands, made nationalism the central force of European politics, and put democracy and the rights of workers permanently on the agenda. A.J.P. A famous verdict calls 1848 'the turning point at which modern history failed to turn.'
aliases: The View from the Habsburg Empire

title: The Rigveda
summary: The oldest of the four Vedas and among the oldest scriptures still in religious use, the oldest layer of Hindu sacred literature: a collection of 1,028 hymns in archaic Sanskrit, addressed to the gods of the Vedic pantheon and chanted as the liturgy of the fire sacrifice. Composed in the second millennium BCE by Indo-Aryan-speaking poets, it was preserved orally with extraordinary precision for centuries before writing, and for millennia after as a living recitation. It is the fountainhead of Hinduism and a window into a vanished world of cattle-herders and charioteers, without the temples or the great later gods of Hinduism.
indexTerms: Jamison, Stephanie W., and Joel P. Brereton
Jamison, Stephanie W., and Michael Witzel
Stephanie W. Jamison and Michael Witzel
Stephanie W. Jamison and Joel P. Brereton

title: The Roman Empire
summary: The political structure that unified the Mediterranean world for five centuries, produced the legal, administrative, and infrastructural inheritance on which European and Near Eastern civilization was built, and whose legacy — in law, language, religion, and institutional imagination — has never fully ended

title: The Roman Republic
summary: The political system through which Rome governed itself for nearly five centuries — replacing monarchy with elected magistrates, a senate, and popular assemblies — before collapsing under the weight of its own military success into the civil wars that produced the empire, and bequeathing its institutional vocabulary to the founders of every subsequent republic.
indexTerms: Hamilton, Alexander; Madison, James; Jay, John

title: The Russian Empire
summary: The vast, cold, autocratic realm that grew from a Moscow principality into the largest contiguous land empire of its age, dragged itself toward Western power by force from above, rested on the bound labor of its serfs, and collapsed in 1917 under the weight of all it had refused to reform.
aliases: Russia as a European great power
The empire seen from below and outside

title: The Russian Revolution
summary: The collapse of Tsarist Russia in 1917 and the Bolshevik seizure of power — producing, after a devastating civil war, the Soviet Union, the world's first communist state, and reshaping global politics for the remainder of the 20th century.
aliases: The October Revolution as Historical Necessity
The October Coup and Its Victims

title: The Rwandan Genocide
summary: The organized mass killing of Rwanda's Tutsi minority and moderate Hutu by the Hutu-dominated government and its militia networks between April and July 1994 — an estimated 800,000 to 1 million people killed in 100 days, the fastest genocide in recorded history, carried out with machetes and local administrative structures while the United Nations force present in Rwanda watched and the world's governments deliberately avoided the word 'genocide' to evade the obligation to act.

title: The Safavid Empire
summary: The Iranian empire that ruled from 1501 to 1736 CE — founded by the teenage Shah Ismail I, who seized Tabriz and proclaimed himself shah at the head of the Qizilbash, the Turkmen tribal cavalry sworn to a militant Sufi order from Ardabil. The Safavids made Twelver Shi'a Islam the state religion of Iran, a transformation that has lasted into the present and that set them against the Sunni Ottoman Empire to the west and the Uzbeks to the east. Crushed by Ottoman firearms at Chaldiran in 1514, the dynasty reached its height under Shah Abbas I, who rebuilt the army, made Isfahan a celebrated capital, and tied the empire to the silk trade, before the state fell to Afghan invaders in 1722 and finally to Nadir Shah in 1736.
aliases: The Shadow of God on Earth

title: The Sahara
summary: The largest hot desert on Earth — a swath of sand, rock, and gravel covering roughly nine million square kilometers across northern Africa — that has split a continent, was once green savanna within the span of human habitation, and continues to shape the world far beyond its sands: driving global climate, fertilizing distant ecosystems, and marking the route that thousands of people now risk everything to cross.
aliases: The desert as home, not barrier
The Sahara as global climate machine
indexTerms: deMenocal, Peter B. and Tierney, Jessica E.
Marq de Villiers and Sheila Hirtle
Lee R. Berger and Marc Aronson

title: The Sahel
summary: The broad semi-arid grassland belt that runs across Africa just south of the Sahara, from the Atlantic to the Red Sea — a fragile transition zone whose grasslands fed great empires, whose position made it the meeting point of desert and farmland, and whose rainfall now makes it a front line of drought, climate stress, and conflict.
aliases: The Shore Where the Caravans Landed
The Front Line of Drought and Conflict
indexTerms: Mary Tiffen, Michael Mortimore, and Francis Gichuki
Stephen Baines and Christian Bouquet (and contributors)

title: The Samurai
summary: The warrior class that seized power from Japan's imperial court and hardened over time into a hereditary estate, ruling the country for some seven hundred years, from the age of the shoguns until the Meiji Restoration abolished it in 1868. Pragmatic professional soldiers who fought brutal civil wars and switched sides for advantage, they were reborn after their abolition as a global legend of honour, loyalty, and the beautiful death -- a code called bushido that was largely codified later, in peacetime and in the modern era, and was harnessed by the militarist state of the 1930s and 1940s.

title: The San
summary: The San peoples of southern Africa, hunter-gatherers of the Kalahari and beyond, whose languages, often loosely grouped as 'Khoisan,' carry the click consonants, whose rock art is among the oldest and richest on earth, and whose genetic lineages branch near the root of the human family tree — a living people long flattened into a fantasy of the timeless primitive, and long dispossessed by farmers, colonists, and conservation states alike.
aliases: A Present People, Not a Past

title: The Sasanian Empire
summary: The last great Persian empire before Islam, ruling Iran and Mesopotamia from 224 to 651 CE. Founded by Ardashir I, who overthrew the Parthians, the Sasanians made Zoroastrianism a state religion served by a powerful priesthood, built a capital at Ctesiphon, and fought Rome and then the Byzantine Empire for four centuries over the Near East. They reached their height under Khosrow I and Khosrow II, exhausted themselves in a final ruinous war with Byzantium, and were conquered by Arab Muslim armies in the 630s and 640s; the last shah, Yazdegerd III, was killed in 651. Persian administrative, artistic, and courtly forms then deeply shaped Islamic civilization.
aliases: The Glory of the Just Kings

title: The Satavahana Dynasty
summary: The first major state of the Deccan, the great plateau of peninsular India, which bridged the gap between the Mauryan collapse and the later kingdoms and grew rich where its Deccan routes fed a booming trade between the Roman world and the East.
aliases: The Drain of Gold to the East

title: The Satellite
summary: The artificial satellite is a machine placed in orbit to relay signals and watch the Earth — a vantage point above the planet that rewired global communication, weather forecasting, war, and surveillance. From Sputnik's launch in 1957 through the dense constellations overhead today, satellites became the hidden infrastructure behind live television, the daily weather forecast, precision warfare, and the surveillance state, all drawing on the same god's-eye view of the planet.
aliases: The High Ground Belongs to Others
The Eye Does Not Ask Permission

title: The Scientific Revolution
summary: A transformation in how European thinkers understood and investigated the natural world — running from Copernicus's heliocentric model of the solar system to Newton's laws of motion — that established the experimental method, mathematical description of nature, and the idea of a law-governed universe as the foundations of modern science.

title: The Scramble for Africa
summary: The rapid partition of Africa by European powers between roughly 1880 and 1914 — in which a continent that was 10% European-controlled became 90% European-controlled in three decades — establishing borders that ignored existing political and ethnic realities and creating the colonial structures whose consequences shaped the 20th century and whose legacy continues to define African politics.
aliases: The Colonial Fork in the Road

title: The Scythians
summary: Among the first great lords of the Eurasian steppe — a loose family of Iranian-speaking horse nomads who, from roughly the eighth to the third century BCE, dominated the Pontic steppe, while related Saka peoples ranged far into Central Asia. Living in wagons and fighting as mounted archers, they buried their kings under great mounds heaped with golden treasure, frustrated invasions by settled empires, and helped pioneer the mounted-archer steppe style that later peoples, from the Huns to the Mongols, would inherit and transform.

title: The Search Engine
summary: The machine that made the web findable -- by crawling, indexing, and above all ranking billions of pages so a query returns a usable result in milliseconds. Google's PageRank, introduced around 1998, treated incoming links as votes of authority and turned ranking into the central problem of the web. That ranking became a gatekeeper: what appears on the first page of results is seen; what does not barely exists. The search engine is not a neutral window onto the web but an editorial machine whose ordering shapes what billions of people can find and therefore believe.
aliases: The Gatekeeper That Was Never Asked to Govern
The Most Efficient Market for Attention Ever Built

title: The Second Temple
summary: The sanctuary that stood in Jerusalem from roughly 516 BCE until Rome destroyed it in 70 CE — the center of Jewish sacrifice, priesthood, and pilgrimage, whose loss forced Judaism to remake itself around prayer, study, and law rather than a single holy building.
aliases: The Temple in the Story of Jesus

title: The Second World War
summary: The deadliest conflict in human history -- killing between 70 and 85 million people across six years of fighting -- producing the Holocaust, the nuclear age, the Cold War, decolonization, and the international order that still governs global politics.

title: The Seljuk Empire
summary: The Great Seljuk Empire (c. 1037–1194 CE) was a Sunni Muslim empire of the Seljuk Turks, a clan of Oghuz Turkic origin, that came to rule Iran, Iraq, and much of the Near East. Its leader Tughril Beg entered Baghdad in 1055 and took the title sultan, holding real power as protector of the Sunni Abbasid caliph, who kept religious authority. Under the great vizier Nizam al-Mulk it built a network of madrasas and a Persianized administration; at Manzikert in 1071 Alp Arslan defeated the Byzantine emperor and opened Anatolia to Turkic settlement. The Great Seljuk line in Iran was ended by the Khwarazmians in 1194, while successor states such as the Sultanate of Rum in Anatolia lasted into the age of the Mongols.

title: The Seneca Falls Convention
summary: The first women's rights convention in American history, held over two days in July 1848 in a chapel in the village of Seneca Falls, New York, where a few hundred reformers, most of them women, adopted a Declaration of Sentiments. Written mainly by Elizabeth Cady Stanton and deliberately modeled on the Declaration of Independence, it proclaimed that all men and women are created equal and demanded for women the rights and the vote that the republic denied them. Organized by five abolitionist reformers — Elizabeth Cady Stanton, the Quaker Lucretia Mott, Martha Coffin Wright, Mary Ann McClintock, and Jane Hunt — and supported powerfully by the Black abolitionist Frederick Douglass, the convention launched the long American struggle for women's equality — though the movement it began was largely white and middle-class, and would later split bitterly over Black male suffrage.
aliases: The room they were not in
indexTerms: Stanton, Elizabeth Cady, Anthony, Susan B. and Gage, Matilda Joslyn (eds.)

title: The September 11 Attacks
summary: The al-Qaeda attacks of September 11, 2001 — four hijacked airliners, the collapse of the World Trade Center's twin towers, a strike on the Pentagon, and a fourth plane forced down in Pennsylvania — killing roughly 2,977 people and triggering two decades of American-led wars, the construction of a domestic surveillance and security state, and a global transformation of how governments justified coercion in the name of safety.
indexTerms: National Commission on Terrorist Attacks upon the United States
Watson Institute, Brown University (Costs of War project)

title: The Shahnameh
summary: The national epic of Greater Iran, the "Book of Kings," composed by the poet Abolqasem Ferdowsi of Tus over roughly three decades and completed around 1010 CE. Running to some 50,000 rhyming couplets, it is among the longest epic poems ever written by a single author. It carries the mythical and legendary past of Iran from the creation of the world through some fifty reigns down to the Arab-Muslim conquest of the Sasanian Empire. Famous for using very little Arabic vocabulary, the poem is widely credited with helping preserve the Persian language and a distinct Iranian identity within the Islamic world, and it became the supreme subject of Persian miniature painting and oral storytelling.
aliases: The Poet Who Saved a Language

title: The Silk Road
summary: The overland and maritime trade network connecting China to the Mediterranean world — the primary channel through which goods, religions, technologies, diseases, and ideas moved across Eurasia for over a millennium.
aliases: The Western Regions as Tributary and Commercial Frontier

title: The Social Contract
summary: The political theory that legitimate government rests on agreement — explicit or implicit — among the people it governs, developed by Hobbes, Locke, and Rousseau into the foundational framework of modern constitutional democracy, and contested ever since on questions of who actually agreed, what was agreed to, and whether the theory can justify political obligation at all.

title: The Sogdians
summary: The Sogdians were an Eastern Iranian-speaking people of Sogdiana, the rich land around Samarkand and Bukhara in present-day Uzbekistan and Tajikistan. They lived not in one kingdom but in a patchwork of independent oasis city-states, usually under the loose overlordship of larger powers — Persians, then Turks, then Arabs. From roughly the fourth to the eighth century CE they were the great merchants of the overland Silk Road, organizing its caravans and founding a diaspora of merchant colonies all the way into China. Sogdian served as a common trading language of the routes, and Sogdian traders and translators carried goods, religions, and alphabets across half of Eurasia. The Arab conquest and the rise of other routes slowly absorbed them, yet historians now see them as the indispensable connectors who made the Silk Road work.
indexTerms: de la Vaissière, Étienne and Trombert, Éric (eds.)

title: The Sokoto Caliphate
summary: The Sokoto Caliphate (1804–1903) was the vast Islamic state of the central Sudan, in what is now northern Nigeria and beyond. Founded by the Fulani scholar Usman dan Fodio through a jihad against Hausa kingdoms he judged corrupt and only nominally Muslim, it became one of the largest and most populous states in nineteenth-century Africa: a confederation of emirates bound by Islamic law and learning, sustained by farming and one of the largest systems of slavery in the modern world. The British conquered it in 1903 and then ruled through its emirs, so that its institutions and the Sultan of Sokoto's authority outlasted the empire itself.
aliases: The caliphate as a renewal of the faith
The caliphate seen from the plantation
The caliphate as a system to be inherited
indexTerms: Lovejoy, Paul E. and Hogendorn, Jan S.
Bello, Muhammed (trans. and ed. in modern editions)
Toyin Falola and Matthew M. Heaton
Paul E. Lovejoy and Jan S. Hogendorn

title: The Song Dynasty
summary: The Song Dynasty (960–1279 CE) produced more technological and economic innovation than any pre-modern society — printing, gunpowder weapons, the magnetic compass, paper money, proto-industrial iron production — without any of it compounding into an industrial revolution. Understanding why is one of the central questions in world history.
aliases: The Song as prey and model
The Song from the maritime periphery

title: The Songhai Empire
summary: The Songhai Empire (c. 1464–1591) was the largest state in West African history — controlling the central node of trans-Saharan trade, hosting one of the medieval world’s largest universities at Timbuktu, and governing a territory larger than Western Europe. Understanding what Songhai was as a political and commercial civilization is the entry’s purpose.
aliases: The trans-Saharan trade from the middle
Timbuktu from North Africa and Europe
indexTerms: Jeppie, Shamil and Diagne, Souleymane Bachir, eds.
Marq de Villiers and Sheila Hirtle
Shamil Jeppie and Souleymane Bachir Diagne (eds.)

title: The Souls of Black Folk
summary: W.E.B. Du Bois's 1903 book of fourteen essays that named the wound of being Black in America — declaring that 'the problem of the twentieth century is the problem of the color line,' giving the world the idea of 'double consciousness' and the 'veil,' and openly challenging Booker T. Washington's accommodation with a demand for full civil rights, the vote, and higher education led by a 'Talented Tenth.'
indexTerms: W.E.B. Du Bois, ed. Henry Louis Gates Jr. and Terri Hume Oliver

title: The Soviet Union under Stalin
summary: The period of Soviet history from Lenin's death through Stalin's own death — encompassing forced collectivization, industrialization, the Great Terror, the Second World War, and the Cold War's beginning — during which the USSR became an industrial superpower and a totalitarian state responsible for millions of deaths.
aliases: One of the Century's Killing Fields
indexTerms: Gregory, Paul R.; Stuart, Robert C.

title: The Space Race
summary: The superpower competition in spaceflight between the United States and the Soviet Union from roughly 1955 to 1975 — beginning with Sputnik's shock in October 1957, cresting with Neil Armstrong's footstep on the Moon in July 1969, and closing with the Apollo–Soyuz handshake in 1975 — a contest that was simultaneously a proxy of nuclear rivalry, a propaganda battle over which system could master nature, and the founding moment of the technological-managerial state.

title: The Spanish Civil War
summary: The war fought in Spain from 1936 to 1939 between the elected Republican government and a Nationalist military rebellion — drawing in fascist Italy and Nazi Germany on one side and the Soviet Union and international volunteers on the other, ending in Franco's dictatorship and functioning as the defining ideological conflict of the 1930s for a generation of European and American writers, intellectuals, and political actors.
aliases: The War That Could Have Stopped Fascism
indexTerms: Radosh, Ronald; Habeck, Mary; Sevostianov, Grigory

title: The Spanish Conquest of the Americas
summary: The decades-long process by which Spanish expeditions toppled the largest states in the Americas — the Aztec Triple Alliance in 1521 and the Inca realm after 1532 — and built an empire across two continents. The conquest ran less on European weaponry than on epidemic disease, the political fractures inside Indigenous empires, and the tens of thousands of Indigenous allies who fought alongside the newcomers. Out of it came the encomienda labor system, a vast silver economy, and a tangle of conquest myths that historians have spent decades dismantling.
aliases: The Spanish Conquest of the Americas
The Conquest of Mexico and Peru

title: The Spread of Islam in Southeast Asia
summary: The gradual conversion of maritime Southeast Asia to Islam over roughly four centuries, carried not by conquest but by merchants, mystics, and marriage along the monsoon trade routes. The first firmly attested Muslim states appeared in northern Sumatra by the late thirteenth century; the faith spread through the great port of Malacca and across the archipelago as rulers converted and their subjects followed, displacing the older Hindu-Buddhist order. The process made Indonesia, Malaysia, and Brunei Muslim-majority and seeded Muslim communities in the southern Philippines and southern Thailand. Indonesia is today the most populous Muslim-majority country on earth.

title: The Spreadsheet
summary: The grid of rows and columns that recalculates itself — the tool that turned the paper ledger into a live model of any quantitative situation. From VisiCalc in 1979 through Lotus 1-2-3 and Microsoft Excel, the spreadsheet democratized financial modeling and made 'what-if' analysis universal. It also spread fragile, error-ridden models and, in at least one notorious case, shaped economic policy on a faulty foundation.
aliases: The Tool That Sold the Machine
indexTerms: Herndon, Thomas and Ash, Michael and Pollin, Robert
Reinhart, Carmen M. and Rogoff, Kenneth S.
Bergstrom, Carl T. and West, Jevin D.

title: The Standardized Container
summary: A corrugated steel box — twenty or forty feet long, the same dimensions wherever it travels — that turned cargo handling from a labor-intensive puzzle into a mechanical routine and made modern globalization physically possible. Before the container, every ship required armies of dockworkers to hand-load individual crates and barrels. After it, a crane picks up the whole box and sets it on a truck or a train in minutes. By collapsing freight costs, it made it economically rational to scatter production across continents — a transformation whose consequences are still reshaping labor, politics, and the geography of manufacturing.
indexTerms: Bernhofen, Daniel M., Zouheir El-Sahli, and Richard Kneller
Bonacich, Edna and Jake B. Wilson
Edna Bonacich and Jake B. Wilson
Daniel M. Bernhofen, Zouheir El-Sahli, and Richard Kneller

title: The State
summary: The state is the strange achievement of making political power impersonal — of transferring obedience from a person to an office, from a ruler to an institution that persists when rulers change. How that depersonalization happens, what it makes possible, and where it breaks down is the central puzzle of political life.
aliases: The State as the Only Relevant Actor

title: The Steam Engine
summary: The engine that converts heat into mechanical work by using steam pressure — the foundational technology of the Industrial Revolution, which for the first time gave humanity a practical way to convert stored energy (coal) into unlimited mechanical power, breaking the constraint that had bound all previous economies to the limits of human and animal muscle.
aliases: The Making of the Working Class

title: The Steppe
summary: The vast belt of open grassland running almost unbroken across the middle of Eurasia, from the plains of Hungary to the borders of China — too dry across much of its belt for reliable farming, but ideal for pasture and the horse, which for some three thousand years turned the peoples who lived on it into the most mobile warriors on earth and made the steppe at once the great engine of nomad empire and a highway along which goods, faiths, and diseases crossed the continent.

title: The Strait of Malacca
summary: The narrow, shallow channel between Sumatra and the Malay Peninsula, roughly 900 kilometers long and at its tightest only a couple of miles wide. For two thousand years it has been the dominant sea-gate between the Indian Ocean and the South China Sea, the shortest route linking the markets of India, the Middle East, and Europe to those of China and East Asia. Whoever held its key ports could tax a vast share of regional and long-distance commerce, and it remains today one of the busiest and most strategically vital waterways on earth.
indexTerms: Barbara Watson Andaya and Leonard Y. Andaya

title: The Strike
summary: The collective withdrawal of labor — workers stopping work together to force a concession from an employer or a state. The strike is the labor movement's ultimate weapon because it converts the worker's dependence on wages into the employer's dependence on workers: when production halts, the employer loses money and must bargain. Its forms range from the orderly walkout to the factory occupation and the general strike, and its history is a long contest between that leverage and the countermeasures built to break it.
aliases: The Strike as Disorder to Be Managed

title: The Suez Crisis
summary: The 1956 war over the Suez Canal, in which Britain, France, and Israel secretly colluded to attack Egypt, seize the canal zone, and undo Nasser's nationalization — a military success that became a political catastrophe and exposed Britain and France as no longer first-rank world powers.
aliases: Invasion survived, defeat turned to triumph
A success undone by an ally
The new managers of the region
indexTerms: Louis, Wm. Roger and Owen, Roger (eds.)
Wm. Roger Louis and Roger Owen (eds.)

title: The Sugar Plantation Complex
summary: The brutal, factory-like system of growing and refining sugar that spread across the tropical Americas from the fifteenth century onward — large estates that joined cane field to boiling-house and ran them on the forced labor of enslaved Africans. Pioneered on the Atlantic islands of Madeira and São Tomé and then exploded across the Caribbean and Brazil, it turned a costly luxury into a daily European craving and a fortune for its planters. It did so by consuming enslaved lives faster than they could be replaced, becoming the single largest engine of the transatlantic slave trade and one of its deadliest labor regimes and an early model of mass production organized for profit.

title: The Sunni-Shia Split
summary: The major division within Islam, which began as a dispute over who should lead the Muslim community after the Prophet Muhammad's death in 632 CE. Sunnis came to accept the succession of caliphs chosen by the community's leading men, beginning with Abu Bakr. Shia Muslims held that leadership belonged to the Prophet's family, beginning with his cousin and son-in-law Ali. The disagreement deepened through civil war, Ali's caliphate and assassination, and above all the killing of his son Husayn at Karbala in 680, the event that came to define Shia identity. The full split was gradual, unfolding over centuries into distinct schools of law, theology, and devotion, and the relationship between the two branches has varied widely ever since.
aliases: The Wronged Family of the Prophet

title: The Supply Chain
summary: The linked network of firms, processes, and flows — spanning raw materials, components, assembly, transport, and retail — that turns nature into a finished good in a consumer's hands. The supply chain made the modern object a collaborative product of dozens of hands across the planet: astonishingly efficient, quietly brittle, and deeply entangled with questions of labor, power, and political choice.
aliases: The View from the Factory Floor
The Politics of the Hollowed-Out Town
indexTerms: Gereffi, Gary; Humphrey, John; Sturgeon, Timothy
Bonacich, Edna, and Wilson, Jake B.
Edna Bonacich and Jake B. Wilson

title: The Survey
summary: The technique of learning about a whole population by carefully questioning a small, representative sample — a counterintuitive idea that lets a few thousand well-chosen people stand in for millions, and whose results depend entirely on choices about who is asked, how, and in what words.
aliases: The Survey as Instrument of State Knowledge
The Survey as Mirror of Public Opinion
indexTerms: Sudman, Seymour and Bradburn, Norman M.
American Association for Public Opinion Research

title: The Swahili Coast
summary: The chain of city-states along East Africa's Indian Ocean shore — Kilwa, Mombasa, Lamu, Zanzibar — that built a trading civilization from monsoon winds, gold, and Islamic-African urban culture, before the Portuguese arrived with cannon and broke the system open.
aliases: A Node in a Larger Network
indexTerms: Ibn Battuta, translated by H.A.R. Gibb

title: The Sykes-Picot Agreement
summary: The secret 1916 understanding between Britain and France, with Imperial Russian assent, on how to divide the Arab provinces of the Ottoman Empire into spheres of influence and control after an expected Allied victory in the First World War. Negotiated mainly by Sir Mark Sykes and Francois Georges-Picot, it sketched a French northern zone, a British southern zone, and an international Palestine. The deal contradicted wartime British encouragement of Arab independence, and when the Bolsheviks published it in 1917 it became a lasting symbol of Western betrayal and of the colonial origins of the modern Middle East. Scholars caution that the popular claim that it drew today's borders is an oversimplification.

title: The Taíno
summary: The Taíno were the Indigenous people of the Greater Antilles — Hispaniola, Cuba, Puerto Rico, Jamaica, and the Bahamas — and the first Americans Christopher Columbus met when he reached Guanahani in October 1492. Within roughly one generation, enslavement, the encomienda labor system, massacre, and European disease shattered their society and killed the vast majority of them, especially on Hispaniola, in one of the most extreme population collapses in recorded history. For centuries they were declared extinct, but Indigenous Caribbean ancestry closely related to ancient Taíno survives in many modern Caribbean people, Taíno words and foods endure worldwide, and a modern revival movement now asserts a living Indigenous Caribbean identity.
aliases: The invasion from the side that met Columbus
The destruction as a Spaniard came to condemn it
The long view, from declared extinction to reclaiming
indexTerms: Keegan, William F.; Hofman, Corinne L.
William F. Keegan and Corinne L. Hofman

title: The Taiping Rebellion
summary: A vast millenarian uprising in southern and central China, led by Hong Xiuquan, a failed examination candidate who came to believe he was the younger brother of Jesus Christ. From 1850 his movement swelled into what is often called the bloodiest civil war in history. The rebels carved out a rival state, the Taiping Heavenly Kingdom, with its capital at Nanjing, banning opium and foot-binding, proclaiming communal property and land-sharing ideals, and preaching a heterodox Christianity-derived faith. It controlled much of the rich lower Yangtze for over a decade and nearly toppled the Qing dynasty before being crushed in 1864 by provincial Chinese armies, aided late by Western-led forces. The dead, from combat, massacre, famine, and disease, are estimated at perhaps twenty to thirty million or more, among the deadliest conflicts in all of human history.

title: The Taj Mahal
summary: The white marble mausoleum on the Yamuna river at Agra, built between roughly 1632 and 1653 by the Mughal emperor Shah Jahan for his dead wife Mumtaz Mahal — the supreme monument of Mughal architecture, a perfectly proportioned tomb set in a four-part paradise garden, and the building that became the global symbol of love and of India itself.
indexTerms: Begley, Wayne E., and Desai, Z. A.
Wayne E. Begley and Z. A. Desai

title: The Tale of Genji
summary: The early eleventh-century Japanese prose narrative (Genji monogatari) written by the court lady Murasaki Shikibu, often called the world's first novel or first psychological novel. Running to fifty-four chapters and a cast of hundreds, it follows Hikaru Genji, the "shining prince," through a glittering court life of love, political rise and exile, and accumulating loss, then carries the story past his death into the darker Uji chapters of the next generation. Suffused with mono no aware, the bittersweet awareness of impermanence, it is the supreme classic of Japanese literature, though the "first novel" claim, its dates, and parts of its text and authorship remain debated.

title: The Talmud
summary: The vast compendium of Jewish law, lore, and debate that became the core text of rabbinic Judaism after the Hebrew Bible — built in two layers, the concise Mishnah redacted in Roman Palestine around 200 CE and the sprawling Gemara that argues over it, and compiled into two versions, the Jerusalem Talmud and the larger, more authoritative Babylonian Talmud completed in the academies of Sasanian Mesopotamia.

title: The Tang Dynasty
summary: The Tang Dynasty (618–907 CE) was the moment China became a crossroads of Eurasian civilization — absorbing Buddhist philosophy from India, silk technology spreading westward, Persian refugees at court, and Central Asian music in the capital. Its analytical question is what happens when a civilization stops being a destination and becomes a junction.
aliases: Japan as Tang's most deliberate student
The Tang from the crossroads traders
Tang China as the eastern limit of the known world

title: The Tango
summary: The music and dance born in the immigrant slums, docks, and brothels of Buenos Aires and Montevideo on the Río de la Plata in the 1880s, fusing Afro-Rioplatense candombe and milonga with the Cuban habanera and European dance forms into a close, aching embrace voiced by the bandoneón. Despised at home as the music of pimps and the poor until Paris fell in love with it around 1910, it returned as respectable art, reached a golden age through the singer Carlos Gardel, was reinvented as concert music by Astor Piazzolla, and became the unmistakable soul of the Río de la Plata, and especially of Argentina’s image of itself, listed by UNESCO as intangible cultural heritage in 2009.
indexTerms: Simon Collier, Artemis Cooper, María Susana Azzi, and Richard Martin

title: The Tariff
summary: A tax on imported goods — historically the chief revenue source of pre-modern states and the main lever of trade policy — and the site where a government's need for money, the politics of protecting home industries, and the theory of free trade collide.
aliases: The Ladder That Was Kicked Away
indexTerms: Amiti, Mary; Redding, Stephen J.; Weinstein, David E.

title: The Telegraph
summary: The first electrical technology to send complex written information faster than a human could carry it. By pulsing electrical signals along a wire in coded patterns, the telegraph separated communication from transportation for the first time in history. A message that once required days of travel could now arrive in seconds. The network of copper wire that spread across continents and beneath oceans in the nineteenth century remade news, markets, diplomacy, and warfare, laying the foundation for every wired communication system that followed.

title: The Telephone
summary: The device that carried the human voice over a wire — replacing coded signals handled by trained operators with direct conversation between ordinary people, and building the largest machine ever assembled to do it: the network.

title: The Terracotta Army
summary: The buried army of life-sized clay soldiers, horses, and chariots arrayed in battle formation around the unexcavated tomb of Qin Shi Huang, China's First Emperor, near Xi'an — made to guard China’s First Emperor in the afterlife, lost for two thousand years, and struck by farmers digging a well in 1974.
aliases: Mausoleum of the First Qin Emperor
Where Did the Realism Come From?
The Wonder We Cannot Fully Open

title: The Thirty Years' War
summary: The catastrophic conflict that devastated Central Europe from 1618 to 1648 -- beginning as a religious war between Catholics and Protestants and ending as a struggle between European great powers that established the modern system of sovereign states.

title: The Thousand and One Nights
summary: A frame-tale collection of stories in Arabic, known in the West as "The Arabian Nights" (Arabic: Alf Layla wa-Layla). It has no single author, no fixed date, and no settled text. Instead it grew by accretion over many centuries, building on an older and now-lost Persian collection with possible Indian roots, an Arabic Baghdad layer set in the court of Harun al-Rashid, and a later Egyptian layer compiled in Cairo. The frame is famous: the betrayed king Shahryar kills a new wife each dawn until Shahrazad keeps herself alive by telling stories that break off unfinished at sunrise. The work was marginal in the classical Arabic tradition and became a global sensation only through Antoine Galland's French translation (1704–1717), which also introduced "Aladdin" and "Ali Baba," tales now thought to be absent from the core Arabic manuscripts.
aliases: The Tales the Scholars Looked Past
indexTerms: Ulrich Marzolph and Richard van Leeuwen
trans. Yasmine Seale (ed. Paulo Lemos Horta)

title: The Tiananmen Square Protests
summary: The student-led pro-democracy movement that occupied Beijing's Tiananmen Square in spring 1989 — drawing millions of demonstrators across China before the People's Liberation Army cleared the square with live fire on the night of June 3–4, killing an unknown number of people — and the Chinese Communist Party's subsequent erasure of the event from public memory inside China.
indexTerms: Andrew J. Nathan and Perry Link (eds.)

title: The Timurid Empire
summary: The empire of Timur (Tamerlane) and his heirs (1370–1507), the last great conquest-state of the steppe tradition, ruled from Samarkand across Central Asia and Persia. Timur built a vast empire in a storm of massacre, leaving towers of skulls in cities that resisted. Yet the dynasty he founded turned that plunder into one of the supreme flowerings of art, science, and architecture the Islamic world has known — the Timurid Renaissance of Samarkand and Herat, where the astronomer-king Ulugh Beg charted the stars and the Persian miniature reached one of its great high points. Pushed out of Central Asia by the Uzbeks around 1500, the dynasty lived on through Babur, who carried the Timurid inheritance into India and founded the Mughal Empire.
aliases: The Age of Samarkand and Herat
indexTerms: Thomas W. Lentz and Glenn D. Lowry

title: The Toltec
summary: To the later Aztecs, the Toltecs were the wisest and most skilled people who ever lived, the inventors of civilization itself, ruled from the golden city of Tollan by a priest-king who fled into the sunrise vowing to return. Beneath that legend lies a real Postclassic city, Tula, in central Mexico (c. 900–1150 CE), famous for its giant stone warrior columns, its colonnaded halls, and its skull racks. How much the Aztec dream of Tollan matches the archaeological reality of Tula is one of the genuinely unresolved problems of Mesoamerican history.
aliases: The puzzle seen from the south
indexTerms: Kowalski, Jeff Karl and Kristan-Graham, Cynthia (eds.)
Michael D. Coe and Rex Koontz
Jeff Karl Kowalski and Cynthia Kristan-Graham (eds.)

title: The Trail of Tears
summary: The forced removal, during the 1830s, of the Indigenous nations of the American Southeast — the Cherokee, Muscogee (Creek), Choctaw, Chickasaw, and Seminole — from their homelands to territory west of the Mississippi River. Driven by settler hunger for Native land and authorized by the Indian Removal Act of 1830, the expulsions killed thousands on the journey, above all during the Cherokee removal of 1838–39. The nations resisted removal by every lawful means, including a Supreme Court victory the federal government refused to enforce. They endured the removal and rebuilt themselves in the West, and the Cherokee, Muscogee (Creek), Choctaw, Chickasaw, and Seminole nations are self-governing peoples today.
aliases: A Wound Survived, Not an Ending
A Republic Against Its Own Law
indexTerms: Theda Perdue and Michael D. Green

title: The Transatlantic Slave Trade
summary: The commercial and imperial system that transported 12.5 million Africans to the Americas over three and a half centuries
indexTerms: Alan L. Olmstead & Paul W. Rhode

title: The Treasury
summary: The institution that holds, collects, and spends the state's money — the fisc, the counting house of power. Every ambition a government has ever acted on, from war to welfare to roads, has run through the treasury first.
indexTerms: Reinhart, Carmen M. and Rogoff, Kenneth S.

title: The Treaty of Versailles
summary: The peace settlement signed on June 28, 1919, that formally ended the First World War — imposing disarmament, territorial losses, reparations, and the war guilt clause on Germany, establishing the League of Nations, and creating the political conditions that made the rise of Hitler and the Second World War possible, or so the dominant interpretation holds.

title: The Treaty of Waitangi
summary: The short treaty signed in 1840 between the British Crown and around 540 Māori chiefs — written in English and in Māori in versions that say crucially different things, with the English text ceding 'sovereignty' and the Māori text (Te Tiriti) ceding only 'kāwanatanga,' governance, while guaranteeing the chiefs 'tino rangatiratanga,' full authority over their lands and treasures. Dismissed by a court in 1877 as a 'simple nullity,' it was revived from the 1970s through the Waitangi Tribunal and a long process of settlements, and is now treated as New Zealand's founding document and a living, still-contested partnership.
aliases: The Treaty That Did Not Stay Dead

title: The Tunnel
summary: A passage driven through rock, soil, or under water — the bridge's hidden twin, crossing an obstacle not over it but through it, fighting collapse, flood, and bad air to carry roads, railways, water, and people where the surface said no.
indexTerms: Flyvbjerg, Bent; Bruzelius, Nils; and Rothengatter, Werner
Bent Flyvbjerg, Nils Bruzelius, and Werner Rothengatter

title: The Turbine
summary: The bladed spinning machine that converts the energy of moving fluid into rotary motion — and the device through which almost all the world's electricity passes, whether the fuel is coal, gas, nuclear, water, or wind.

title: The U.S. Constitution
summary: The 1787 document that frames the government of the United States, the oldest written national constitution still in force. Drafted in a single Philadelphia summer to replace the failing Articles of Confederation, it created a federal republic in which power is split between the nation and the states and divided among three branches that check one another. It made itself the supreme law of the land, set a deliberately hard amendment process used only twenty-seven times, and was ratified in 1788. Its genius is structural and its flaw is moral: to win the slave states, the framers built protections for slavery into the design without naming it, through the three-fifths clause, the slave-trade clause, and the fugitive-slave clause, and over-weighted small and slave states through the Senate and the Electoral College. It took the Civil War and later amendments to begin correcting that bargain, and Americans still argue over whether to read the text by its framers' meaning or as a living document.
aliases: The Constitution of the United States
The Word They Would Not Write
indexTerms: Constitutional Convention, and the State Ratifying Conventions

title: The Umayyad Caliphate
summary: The first hereditary Islamic dynasty, ruling from 661 to 750 CE with its capital at Damascus — founded by Mu'awiya after the civil war that followed the killing of the caliph Ali. The Umayyads presided over the great Arab-Muslim conquests, carrying their armies across North Africa, into Iberia in 711, and to the edges of India and Central Asia, building one of the largest empires the world had yet seen. They made Arabic the language of government, struck the first distinctly Islamic coinage, and raised the Dome of the Rock in Jerusalem and the Great Mosque of Damascus. They ruled a vast non-Arab subject population, with a growing body of non-Arab converts, while privileging Arabs, and the grievances this bred, together with Shia and Kharijite opposition, helped bring the Abbasid Revolution that overthrew them in 750.
aliases: The Usurpers of the Prophet's House

title: The Underground Railroad
summary: The loose, secret, and largely Black-led web of routes, safe houses, and helpers that aided enslaved people escaping the American South before the Civil War — best understood not as a benevolent white rescue operation but as the visible edge of the enslaved freeing themselves.

title: The United Nations
summary: The standing organization of nearly all the world's states, founded in 1945 from the wreckage of the Second World War to keep peace and coordinate cooperation. It pairs a universal General Assembly, where every state has one vote, with a powerful Security Council whose five permanent members can each veto any action. That built-in compromise is what lets the organization function and what guarantees it will fail whenever a great power blocks it.
aliases: A Forum Won and a Hierarchy Preserved

title: The University
summary: The institutional form created in medieval Europe between roughly 1088 and the 13th century — a corporation of scholars with the right to grant degrees, protected by royal or papal charter — that became the primary institution for the production, preservation, and transmission of knowledge in the modern world, and whose basic structure has proved remarkably durable across nine centuries of radical social change.
indexTerms: Harvard University Committee on Harvard & the Legacy of Slavery
Carrell, Scott E.; West, James E.

title: The Upanishads
summary: The texts where Indian thought turned inward, from the fire sacrifice and the gods of the Vedas to a single radical question: what is the self, and how is it related to the ultimate reality behind everything? Composed in Sanskrit as the final layer of each Veda, the principal Upanishads (roughly a dozen, conventionally dated to about 800–200 BCE, with the count and dates both debated) are not systematic treatises but dialogues and teachings. Their most influential passages identify, or closely relate, the innermost self, atman, with Brahman, the ultimate reality behind everything — a relation later Vedanta schools read in sharply different ways. As Vedanta, "the end of the Veda," they became the seedbed of Vedanta and a great foundation of later Hindu philosophy, and the philosophical heart of Hinduism.

title: The Valladolid Debate
summary: The debate held at Valladolid in Spain in 1550 and 1551, when the Emperor Charles V suspended further conquests in the Americas and convened a junta of theologians to argue whether Spain could justly rule and convert the indigenous peoples of the New World. The Dominican friar Bartolomé de las Casas argued that they were rational human beings with souls, rights, and legitimate societies who should be won by peaceful persuasion; the scholar Juan Ginés de Sepúlveda argued, using Aristotle's idea of natural slavery, that their barbarism and human sacrifice made conquest a just and lesser evil. The debate ended without a clear verdict, both men claimed victory, and the conquest continued — yet it is remembered as one of the first times a European empire formally questioned its own right to conquer.
aliases: An empire examining its own conscience

title: The Vietnam War
summary: A conflict in Southeast Asia in which North Vietnam and its southern allies fought South Vietnam and the United States for two decades, ending in 1975 with communist victory — demonstrating the limits of conventional military superiority against determined guerrilla resistance and transforming American politics, society, and foreign policy.
aliases: The American War and the Completion of Independence
The Lost Country and the Diaspora
The War That Cannot Be Named

title: The Vijayanagara Empire
summary: The last great empire of medieval South India (c. 1336–1646), a vast and fabulously wealthy Hindu-ruled state that dominated the peninsula for over two centuries from its wonder-city on the Tungabhadra river. Grown rich on cotton, spices, diamonds, and imported war horses, it astonished foreign travelers before a coalition of Deccan sultanates broke it at the Battle of Talikota in 1565 and reduced its capital to the ruins now called Hampi.
indexTerms: Richard M. Eaton and Phillip B. Wagoner

title: The Vikings
summary: The seafaring Norse people of Scandinavia who, for nearly three centuries, burst out of the north in their superb longships to raid monasteries, found cities, settle Iceland and Greenland, reach North America, carve out states from Russia to Normandy, and trade in slaves and silver from Baghdad to Newfoundland — far more than the horned barbarians of legend, and far harder to romanticize than the modern myth allows.
aliases: The Vikings as a scourge from the sea
The Vikings as captors and destroyers
indexTerms: Ibn Fadlan (trans. Paul Lunde and Caroline Stone)

title: The Virgin of Guadalupe
summary: Nuestra Señora de Guadalupe: an image of the Mother of God that, the tradition holds, appeared imprinted on a peasant's cloak near Mexico City in 1531, a decade after the Spanish conquest. The dark-skinned, indigenous-featured Virgin who spoke to the conquered in their own language fused the new Catholic faith with the old indigenous world and became, across five centuries, the supreme symbol of Mexico itself: of its faith, its mixed-race identity, and its struggles for freedom. Scholars and some clergy have honestly questioned the apparition's historicity and even Juan Diego's existence; the Church affirms both, and Juan Diego was canonized in 2002. What is undeniable is the devotion: the image hangs in one of the most-visited Catholic shrines in the world.
aliases: The Mother Who Came to the Conquered
A Devotion Made in the Contact Zone
indexTerms: Sousa, Lisa; Poole, Stafford; Lockhart, James (eds. and trans.)
Lisa Sousa, Stafford Poole, and James Lockhart (editors)

title: The Wage
summary: Payment for labor by time or output — the form through which most people in a market economy get their living, and the hinge of the whole employment relationship. The wage looks like a simple price, but it encodes the central bargain of capitalism: what your time is worth, and who decides.
indexTerms: Mishel, Lawrence; Bernstein, Jared; Shierholz, Heidi
Azar, José; Marinescu, Ioana; Steinbaum, Marshall
Anna Stansbury and Lawrence H. Summers
Mishel, Lawrence; Bivens, Josh; Gould, Elise; Shierholz, Heidi

title: The Wahhabi Movement
summary: A Sunni revivalist movement that began in the Najd region of central Arabia in the eighteenth century, preaching a strict return to the absolute oneness of God and the practice of the earliest Muslims. Its founder, Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab, condemned saint veneration, tomb visitation, and much popular devotion as idolatry. A pact with the House of Saud bound the movement to a dynasty that would build the modern Kingdom of Saudi Arabia, from which, in the oil age, its influence spread worldwide.

title: The Warehouse
summary: The building where goods wait — a buffer between production and consumption that makes trade across time and distance possible. From ancient granaries storing against famine to bonded warehouses holding goods duty-unpaid under customs seal, warehouse receipts financing commerce on credit, and the modern fulfillment center run by robots and watched by algorithms: the warehouse is stored time, and whoever controls it controls the tempo of the economy.
aliases: The Warehouse as Buffer Against Uncertainty

title: The Wars of Religion
summary: The long, savage series of conflicts that followed the Protestant Reformation's splitting of Western Christianity, in which Catholics and Protestants slaughtered one another across Europe from roughly the 1520s to 1648 — the German wars of the Reformation, the French Wars of Religion, the Dutch Revolt, and the catastrophic Thirty Years' War. The killing ended not in the victory of one faith but in exhaustion. Many historians argue that out of that exhaustion came a gradual loosening of religious uniformity and the principle of state sovereignty, partly settled at the Peace of Westphalia in 1648.
aliases: The View from the Confessional Divide

title: The Wealth of Nations
summary: Adam Smith's 1776 investigation into the causes of national prosperity -- the foundational text of modern economics, which established that markets coordinate economic activity more effectively than government direction, and whose arguments have been used to justify both the free market policies it recommends and the social concerns it actually expresses.
aliases: An Inquiry into the Nature and Causes of the Wealth of Nations
Smith as the Prophet of Capitalism
Smith as the Starting Point for Critique
Smith as the First Development Economist

title: The Weimar Republic
summary: The German democratic republic established after the First World War's defeat, named for the city where its constitution was written, that lasted fourteen years before being legally dissolved by Hitler's appointment as Chancellor — a democracy that failed under the combined weight of economic catastrophe, political polarization, institutional weakness, and the deliberate assault of its enemies.
aliases: The Republic of the November Criminals

title: The Welfare State
summary: The set of institutions through which modern states assume collective responsibility for the basic economic security of their citizens — providing income support, healthcare, housing, and education as social rights rather than market goods or charitable provision. The welfare state is the 20th century's most consequential domestic policy innovation and its most contested: it transformed the relationship between citizens and states while generating enduring disagreements about its proper scope, costs, and effects.
aliases: The Welfare State as Dependency Trap
The Welfare State as Gendered Institution

title: The Wheel
summary: The proverbial benchmark of invention — a disc rotating on a fixed axle — arrived surprisingly late, spread from a narrow Eurasian origin, and was useless without roads, draft animals, and the engineering insight that the hard part was not the wheel but the axle. Entire civilizations thrived without it, and some abandoned it deliberately. Its deeper legacy is rotary motion itself: the principle that made gears, millstones, turbines, and engines possible.
indexTerms: Littauer, Mary A. and Crouwel, Joost H.
Mary A. Littauer and Joost H. Crouwel

title: The Witch Hunts
summary: The wave of witch trials that swept Christian Europe and its colonies from roughly 1450 to 1750, in which courts prosecuted people for an imaginary crime — a pact with the Devil to work harm by magic. Local grudges, misfortune, and fear produced accusations; torture produced confessions and named further suspects; and tens of thousands of people, most of them women, were executed. The death toll has been badly mythologized: serious historians estimate roughly 40,000 to 60,000 executions, not the 'nine million' of popular legend.

title: The Wretched of the Earth
summary: Frantz Fanon's 1961 analysis of colonialism, decolonization, and the psychology of the colonized — arguing that colonial violence could only be overcome through counter-violence, that the colonized must create new national cultures rather than imitating Europe, and that the pitfalls of national consciousness threatened to reproduce colonial structures after independence.

title: The Xiongnu
summary: The first great nomad confederation of the eastern steppe. Modu forged this mounted empire around 209 BCE, and it grew so dangerous that the newly unified Chinese empire extended frontier walls and bought peace with marriage diplomacy, silk, and grain for generations. Han pressure, costly campaigns, diplomacy, and Xiongnu internal divisions eventually broke the confederation apart. That struggle hardened China's frontier and sent envoys west, opening the Silk Road. It also set the template of the steppe empire (the chanyu, the dual wings, the marriage-tribute relationship) that the Göktürks and Mongols would inherit. Almost everything known about the Xiongnu comes from hostile Chinese sources, and their language, ethnic identity, and possible later link to Attila's Huns all remain genuinely contested.

title: The Young Turks
